key_discoveries: Identification of the first quasar (3C 273) and proof that its huge redshift indicates cosmological distance and colossal luminosity.
Discovery of the class of quasars as active nuclei of distant galaxies, enabling the study of the early Universe.
Development of spectroscopic methods for studying distant objects and their redshifts.
Maarten Schmidt.quote: When I realized that the lines in the spectrum of 3C 273 were ordinary hydrogen lines, only shifted by 15%, I felt as if I had been struck by lightning. This meant the object was billions of light-years away from us.
Maarten Schmidt.fun_fact: Schmidt had long been unable to understand the spectrum of 3C 273 until he realized that the Balmer series lines were strongly redshifted. The insight came to him while driving home. He later said that the discovery process was like assembling a puzzle without a picture — the pieces suddenly came together into a single image.

Paul Dirac.name: Paul Dirac
Paul Dirac.description: British theoretical physicist, one of the founders of quantum mechanics. He formulated the Dirac equation, which united special relativity and quantum theory, and predicted the existence of antimatter. His work laid the foundation for quantum electrodynamics and field theory.
Paul Dirac.biography: Born in Bristol, England. Studied electrical engineering at the University of Bristol, then mathematics at Cambridge. Completed his doctoral thesis under Ralph Fowler. At age 30, became the Lucasian Professor of Mathematics at Cambridge (a position once held by Isaac Newton). Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933 jointly with Erwin Schrödinger. After retiring, moved to Florida, where he taught at Florida State University. Fellow of the Royal Society, member of the Order of Merit. Known for his extreme taciturnity and literal perception of the world.
key_discoveries: Derivation of the Dirac equation (1928), which describes the behavior of relativistic fermions and predicts the existence of antiparticles (the positron was discovered in 1932).
Development of Fermi-Dirac statistics for particles with half-integer spin.
Introduction of the Dirac delta function and bra-ket notation, which became standard tools of quantum mechanics.
Foundational work on quantum electrodynamics and the theory of magnetic monopoles.
Paul Dirac.quote: The beauty of equations is more important than their agreement with experiments. If an equation is not beautiful, it is probably wrong.
Paul Dirac.fun_fact: Dirac was known for his extreme reticence. Once after a lecture a student said, 'Professor Dirac, I didn't understand the last equation.' Dirac simply remained silent. When reminded of the question, he replied, 'That was not a question, it was a statement.' He also remained unmarried for a long time, but one day met the sister of a colleague and unusually for him said, 'You are my better half.' They lived together for the rest of his life.

Peter Higgs.name: Peter Higgs
Peter Higgs.description: British theoretical physicist who predicted the existence of the elementary particle — the Higgs boson, responsible for the mechanism of spontaneous electroweak symmetry breaking. The experimental discovery of the boson at the Large Hadron Collider in 2012 confirmed his theory.
Peter Higgs.biography: Studied at King's College London. Worked at the University of Edinburgh. Nobel Prize in Physics (2013) jointly with François Englert.
key_discoveries: Prediction of the Higgs mechanism and the Higgs boson (1964)
Peter Higgs.quote: The Higgs boson is part of the mechanism that gives other particles mass, and its existence proves that our understanding of the nature of symmetries is correct.
Peter Higgs.fun_fact: Peter Higgs disliked the boson being called the 'God particle'; he preferred the technical name.

Brian Schmidt.name: Brian Schmidt
Brian Schmidt.description: Australian-American astrophysicist, Nobel laureate in Physics (2011) for the discovery of the accelerated expansion of the Universe through observations of distant supernovae. His work led to the concept of dark energy.
Brian Schmidt.biography: Studied at the University of Arizona and Harvard. Worked at the Australian National University, leads the school of astronomy and astrophysics. Actively engaged in science popularization and winemaking.
key_discoveries: Discovery of the acceleration of the Universe's expansion (1998) from Type Ia supernova data
Brian Schmidt.quote: The Universe is not just expanding, it is expanding faster and faster, and we don't know what causes this acceleration.
Brian Schmidt.fun_fact: In addition to astrophysics, Brian Schmidt enjoys winemaking and owns a winery in Australia.

Hans Bethe.name: Hans Bethe
Hans Bethe.description: German-American physicist, Nobel Prize laureate (1967) for his work on the theory of nuclear reactions, especially for the discovery of energy sources in stars (proton-proton cycle and CNO cycle). His work underpins the understanding of stellar nucleosynthesis.
Hans Bethe.biography: He studied in Frankfurt and Munich, emigrated to the United States. He worked at Cornell University, participated in the Manhattan Project. He contributed to many areas of physics.
key_discoveries: Establishment of two main chains of thermonuclear reactions in stars (1938–1939)
Bethe-Bloch formula for energy loss of charged particles
Important contribution to quantum electrodynamics
Hans Bethe.quote: Stars are giant thermonuclear reactors, and we have learned to understand how they shine.
Hans Bethe.fun_fact: Bethe was known for his longevity in science: he actively worked and published until he was over 90, working on neutrino astrophysics, among other things.

Fred Lawrence Whipple.name: Fred Lawrence Whipple
Fred Lawrence Whipple.description: American astronomer who proposed the 'dirty snowball' model for comet nuclei, which explained their activity when approaching the Sun. He also contributed to the discovery of asteroids and the study of meteors.
Fred Lawrence Whipple.biography: He studied at the University of California, worked at the Harvard College Observatory and the Smithsonian Astrophysical Observatory. Director of the SAO, mentor to many astronomers.
key_discoveries: Model of a comet nucleus as a conglomerate of ice and dust (1950)
Discovery of several asteroids and comets
Invention of a protective shield against meteoroid hazards for spacecraft ('Whipple shield')
Fred Lawrence Whipple.quote: A comet is a dirty snowball, evaporating in the sun's heat.
Fred Lawrence Whipple.fun_fact: During World War II, Whipple worked on secret projects, including the development of 'chaff' — metalized paper dropped to jam enemy radar.

Nancy Grace Roman.name: Nancy Grace Roman
Nancy Grace Roman.description: American astronomer known as the 'Mother of Hubble' for tirelessly championing the idea of space telescopes. She played a key role in planning and implementing the Hubble project, as well as in programs to study galaxies and stellar populations.
Nancy Grace Roman.biography: She studied at Swarthmore College and the University of Chicago. She worked at NASA, where she became the first woman in a leadership position in the field of astronomy. She contributed to the development of satellite observatories.
key_discoveries: Pioneering work on the study of the chemical composition of stars depending on their position in the Galaxy
Role in the creation of the Hubble Space Telescope
Nancy Grace Roman.quote: If I hadn't become an astronomer, I would probably have become a lawyer — I always had a gift for persuasion.
Nancy Grace Roman.fun_fact: Roman was interested in astronomy from childhood and even organized an astronomy club at school, despite a lack of support.

Rashid Alievich Sunyaev.name: Rashid Alievich Sunyaev
Rashid Alievich Sunyaev.description: Soviet and Russian astrophysicist, known for predicting the Sunyaev-Zel'dovich effect, which allows observing galaxy clusters through distortions of the cosmic microwave background. He made fundamental contributions to relativistic astrophysics and accretion theory.
Rashid Alievich Sunyaev.biography: He studied at the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology, a student of Yakov Zel'dovich. He worked at the Space Research Institute of the Russian Academy of Sciences, head of sector. Laureate of numerous international awards, including the Breakthrough Prize in Fundamental Physics.
key_discoveries: Prediction of the Sunyaev-Zel'dovich effect (1972) — scattering of the cosmic microwave background on hot gas in galaxy clusters
Standard accretion disk (Shakura-Sunyaev model)
Rashid Alievich Sunyaev.quote: The cosmic microwave background is a window into the history of the Universe, and galaxy clusters leave their imprint on it.
Rashid Alievich Sunyaev.fun_fact: The Sunyaev-Zel'dovich effect allowed astronomers to create catalogs of galaxy clusters visible from the farthest distances.

Niels Bohr.name: Niels Bohr
Niels Bohr.description: Danish theoretical physicist, creator of the quantum atomic model that explained the spectral lines of hydrogen (Bohr model). He made a decisive contribution to the development of quantum mechanics and its interpretation (Copenhagen interpretation). Debated with Einstein on the completeness of quantum theory.
Niels Bohr.biography: He studied at the University of Copenhagen, then worked at the Manchester laboratory under Rutherford. Founded the Institute for Theoretical Physics in Copenhagen. Nobel laureate in physics (1922).
key_discoveries: Planetary model of the atom with quantized orbits (1913)
Correspondence principle
Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics
Niels Bohr.quote: The opposite of a correct statement is a false statement. But the opposite of a profound truth may be another profound truth.
Niels Bohr.fun_fact: During World War II, Bohr was secretly taken out of occupied Denmark on an American bomber, where he had to sit in the bomb bay.

Albert A. Michelson.name: Albert A. Michelson
Albert A. Michelson.description: American physicist known for his precise measurements of the speed of light and the creation of the interferometer, which became the basis for many optical experiments, including the Michelson-Morley experiment. His work laid the foundation for the special theory of relativity.
Albert A. Michelson.biography: He studied at the US Naval Academy, then at the Universities of Berlin and Heidelberg. He worked at the Case School of Applied Science, Clark University, and the University of Chicago. He was the first American scientist to receive the Nobel Prize in Physics (1907) for his precise optical instruments and the spectroscopic and metrological investigations carried out with their aid.
key_discoveries: High-precision measurement of the speed of light (1879–1883)
Invention of the Michelson interferometer (1881)
Michelson-Morley experiment (1887), demonstrating the absence of ether wind
Determination of star diameters using interferometry (1920)
Albert A. Michelson.quote: Future discoveries await us in the sixth decimal place.
Albert A. Michelson.fun_fact: Michelson measured the speed of light throughout his life, constantly improving accuracy, and his last measurement was completed after his death.

Robert Woodrow Wilson.name: Robert Woodrow Wilson
Robert Woodrow Wilson.description: American radio astronomer, Nobel laureate in Physics (1978) for the joint discovery with Arno Penzias of the cosmic microwave background radiation, which became a key piece of evidence for the Big Bang theory.
Robert Woodrow Wilson.biography: He studied at Rice University and the California Institute of Technology. He worked at Bell Laboratories, where together with Penzias, using a horn antenna, he discovered the cosmic microwave background radiation.
key_discoveries: Discovery of the cosmic microwave background radiation (1965)
Robert Woodrow Wilson.quote: We weren't looking for it; it found us.
Robert Woodrow Wilson.fun_fact: Initially, Wilson and Penzias thought that the noise in the antenna was caused by pigeon droppings and spent time cleaning it before realizing the nature of the signal.

Theodore Lyman.name: Theodore Lyman
Theodore Lyman.description: American physicist who discovered the ultraviolet series of hydrogen (the Lyman series), widely used in astrophysics to study the interstellar and intergalactic medium, as well as in cosmology (Lyman-alpha forest).
Theodore Lyman.biography: He studied at Harvard University, where he later worked. He headed the physics laboratory at Harvard and worked on spectroscopy.
key_discoveries: Discovery of the Lyman series of hydrogen (1906–1914)
Research in ultraviolet spectroscopy
Theodore Lyman.quote: The true value of a discovery is sometimes not immediately visible; it only becomes apparent over time when others find applications for it.
Theodore Lyman.fun_fact: Lyman was a wealthy man and paid for some of the equipment for his ultraviolet research out of his own pocket.

Alan Guth.name: Alan Guth
Alan Guth.description: American theoretical physicist, creator of the inflationary model of the universe, which explains the large-scale homogeneity and flatness of the universe, and also generates the initial perturbations that led to the formation of galaxies.
Alan Guth.biography: He studied at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, and worked at Princeton, Columbia, and Stanford universities, as well as at MIT. He was awarded the Dirac Medal and other prizes.
key_discoveries: Development of the inflationary stage in the early universe (1981)
Explanation of the horizon and flatness problems
Prediction of the spectrum of primordial perturbations
Alan Guth.quote: The inflationary model is often called the 'free lunch' theory because it allows a huge universe to arise almost from nothing.
Alan Guth.fun_fact: Guth came up with inflation when he was 32 years old, and the idea came to him while listening to a lecture on problems in cosmology.

David Charbonneau.name: David Charbonneau
David Charbonneau.description: American astronomer, one of the pioneers of the transit method for detecting exoplanets. He led the group that first recorded the transit of an exoplanet across the disk of a star (HD 209458 b), opening a new era in the study of planetary systems.
David Charbonneau.biography: He studied at the University of Toronto and Harvard University. He is a professor at Harvard University, a leading researcher in the field of exoplanet transits and atmospheres.
key_discoveries: First detection of the transit of exoplanet HD 209458 b (1999)
Research on exoplanet atmospheres
David Charbonneau.quote: When we look at transits of exoplanets, we see the shadows of alien worlds.
David Charbonneau.fun_fact: He earned the nickname 'planet hunter' because his work on transits led to the discovery of hundreds of new worlds.

Serge Haroche.name: Serge Haroche
Serge Haroche.description: French physicist, one of the creators of cavity QED (electrodynamics of resonators). He studies the interaction of single atoms with photons in superconducting cavities, observes quantum decoherence, and implements non-destructive quantum measurements. Nobel Prize laureate in 2012.
Serge Haroche.biography: Born in Casablanca, Morocco. PhD from Pierre and Marie Curie University in Paris. Professor at the Collège de France and École Normale Supérieure. Together with Jean-Michel Raimond, he built a unique experimental setup that allows 'counting' photons without absorbing them.
key_discoveries: Non-destructive quantum measurements — the ability to measure the number of photons in a resonator without destroying them.
Observation of progressive decoherence — direct recording of the process of losing superposition due to interaction with the environment.
Creation and manipulation of 'Schrödinger's cat' states from the radiation field in a resonator.
Serge Haroche.quote: The boundary between the quantum and classical worlds is not a wall, but a bridge that we are only learning to cross.
Serge Haroche.fun_fact: His resonator experiment can be compared to 'counting photons without turning on the light'; it literally allows one to see quantum superposition and its collapse.

David Bohm.name: David Bohm
David Bohm.description: American-British theoretical physicist whose work spans quantum mechanics, plasma theory, and the philosophy of mind. He proposed an interpretation of quantum mechanics with hidden variables (pilot-wave theory) and together with Y. Aharonov predicted the Aharonov–Bohm effect.
David Bohm.biography: Born in Wilkes-Barre, USA. PhD from the University of California, Berkeley. He worked at Princeton University but due to McCarthy-era persecution left the United States. He taught at the University of São Paulo (Brazil) and Birkbeck College in London. His ideas were long rejected but later gained recognition.
key_discoveries: Pilot-wave theory (de Broglie–Bohm) — a deterministic interpretation of quantum mechanics in which particles have trajectories guided by the wave function.
Aharonov–Bohm effect — the influence of electromagnetic potential on a quantum particle even in the absence of a field (predicted in 1959, experimentally confirmed).
Bohm diffusion — anomalously fast transport of particles across a magnetic field in plasma.
David Bohm.quote: Quantum theory requires a new view of reality, in which the whole determines the behavior of the parts, not the other way around.
David Bohm.fun_fact: Due to political persecution, Bohm could not attend the defense of his own dissertation; his advisor Robert Oppenheimer represented him.

Charles Bennett.name: Charles Bennett
Charles Bennett.description: American physicist and information theorist, one of the founders of quantum information theory. Together with G. Brassard, he developed the first quantum key distribution protocol (BB84) and made decisive contributions to the concept of quantum teleportation. He is also known for his work on reversible computing and the thermodynamics of information.
Charles Bennett.biography: Born in New York City. PhD from Harvard. Since 1973 he has worked at IBM Research, where he has spent most of his career. In 1993, together with colleagues, he proposed the quantum teleportation protocol. Wolf Prize laureate in physics (2018) for contributions to quantum information.
key_discoveries: BB84 quantum key distribution — the first practically implementable quantum cryptography protocol, using photon polarization.
Quantum teleportation (jointly with Brassard, Crépeau, Jozsa, Peres, and Wootters) — transfer of an unknown quantum state between two parties using a classical channel and pre-shared entanglement.
Landauer–Bennett principle — the connection between thermodynamics and information, proving that computation can be reversible and does not necessarily dissipate heat.
Charles Bennett.quote: Quantum mechanics gives us not only new physics but also a new way of thinking about the very concept of information.
Charles Bennett.fun_fact: Bennett is interested in biology and film history; he can often be seen at conferences with a camera.

John von Neumann.name: John von Neumann
John von Neumann.description: Hungarian-American mathematician and physicist whose work laid the foundations of quantum mechanics, computer science, and game theory. He gave a mathematically rigorous formulation of quantum theory (in the Hilbert space formalism), introduced the concept of von Neumann entropy, and developed the architecture of modern computers.
John von Neumann.biography: Born in Budapest. PhD from the University of Budapest. Professor at Princeton University and the Institute for Advanced Study. He participated in the Manhattan Project, advised the US government on defense matters. His book 'Mathematical Foundations of Quantum Mechanics' became a classic.
key_discoveries: Hilbert space formalism in quantum mechanics — a rigorous mathematical formulation of the theory.
Von Neumann entropy — the quantum analog of thermodynamic entropy, a measure of the uncertainty of a quantum state.
Von Neumann architecture — the principle of building computers with a stored-program concept, underlying nearly all modern computers.
Game theory — the minimax theorem, laying the foundations of mathematical economics.
John von Neumann.quote: True mathematics is not about imposing exchange value with overhead costs, but about understanding: why is it so?
John von Neumann.fun_fact: He had a phenomenal memory: he could recite entire chapters of books and pages of the telephone directory from memory.

Richard Feynman.name: Richard Feynman
Richard Feynman.description: American theoretical physicist, one of the creators of quantum electrodynamics. He developed the path integral method (Feynman integrals) and the diagram technique (Feynman diagrams), which became the main tools of theoretical physics. He contributed to the theory of weak interactions, superfluidity of helium-3, and particle physics. He formulated the concept of quantum computing long before its technical implementation.
Richard Feynman.biography: Born in New York. He earned his PhD at Princeton. He participated in the Manhattan Project. Professor at Cornell University and the California Institute of Technology. Winner of the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1965 (together with S. Tomonaga and J. Schwinger) for fundamental work in quantum electrodynamics.
key_discoveries: Path integrals — an alternative formulation of quantum mechanics in which a particle 'explores' all possible paths.
Feynman diagrams — a visual and powerful method for calculating interactions in quantum field theory.
The parton model — the idea that hadrons consist of point-like particles (partons), which anticipated the quark model.
The V–A theory of weak interaction — together with M. Gell-Mann, he proposed the vector-axial structure of weak currents.
Richard Feynman.quote: I think I can safely say that nobody understands quantum mechanics.
Richard Feynman.fun_fact: He enjoyed playing the bongo drums and cracking safes; during the Manhattan Project, he amused himself by picking the locks of his colleagues.

John Clauser.name: John Clauser
John Clauser.description: American experimental physicist who was the first to test Bell's inequalities using entangled photons. His experiments refuted local hidden variables and confirmed the nonlocality of quantum mechanics. Winner of the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2022.
John Clauser.biography: Born in Pasadena. He earned his PhD at Columbia University. He worked at the University of California, Berkeley, and the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory. In 1972, together with Stuart Freedman, he performed the famous experiment that demonstrated the violation of Bell's inequalities.
key_discoveries: Experimental test of Bell's inequality — the first convincing proof of quantum nonlocality.
Development of the Clauser–Horne–Shimony–Holt (CHSH) inequality — an experimentally convenient form of Bell's inequality.
John Clauser.quote: I hoped to prove that Einstein was right, but nature ruled otherwise.
John Clauser.fun_fact: In his youth, he built homemade rockets and even won a science fair with a project on a cloud chamber.

Anton Zeilinger.name: Anton Zeilinger
Anton Zeilinger.description: Austrian physicist, pioneer of quantum information and quantum optics. He designed and carried out key experiments on quantum teleportation, multi-particle entanglement, and Bell inequality tests with all local loopholes closed. Winner of the Nobel Prize in 2022.
Anton Zeilinger.biography: Born in Ried (Austria). He earned his doctorate at the University of Vienna. Professor at the University of Innsbruck and the University of Vienna. President of the Austrian Academy of Sciences. He led the group that achieved the first quantum teleportation of an independent qubit and entanglement distribution between the Canary Islands.
key_discoveries: Quantum teleportation — transfer of a quantum state without transferring matter.
Entanglement swapping — creation of entanglement between particles that have never interacted.
Experiments with loophole closure — simultaneous closing of detection and communication loopholes in the Bell test.
Anton Zeilinger.quote: Quantum mechanics teaches us that reality does not exist until we measure it.
Anton Zeilinger.fun_fact: In one experiment, his team teleported photon states between the islands of La Palma and Tenerife over a distance of 143 km.

Roy Glauber.name: Roy Glauber
Roy Glauber.description: American theoretical physicist, considered the founder of quantum optics. He developed the theory of higher-order optical coherence, introduced the concept of coherent states (Glauber states), and explained photon statistics in laser radiation. Winner of the Nobel Prize in 2005.
Roy Glauber.biography: Born in New York. He received his PhD from Harvard. At age 18, he participated in the Manhattan Project. He worked at the Institute for Advanced Study, Caltech, and Harvard University. The Nobel Prize was awarded for his contribution to the quantum theory of optical coherence.
key_discoveries: Quantum theory of optical coherence — mathematical description of light coherence at the quantum level.
Glauber states — quantum states that are closest to classical electromagnetic waves, serving as the basis for describing laser radiation.
Explanation of the Hanbury Brown–Twiss effect — quantum interpretation of photon correlation.
Roy Glauber.quote: Light is not only a wave or a particle: it is something more subtle that we are only beginning to understand with the help of quantum theory.
Roy Glauber.fun_fact: As a student, he was recruited for the Manhattan Project, where at age 18 he calculated nuclear reaction cross sections for the atomic bomb.

David Wineland.name: David Wineland
David Wineland.description: American physicist, pioneer in laser cooling and ion trapping. He developed methods for controlling individual quantum systems, which formed the basis for quantum logic gates and quantum computers. Winner of the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2012.
David Wineland.biography: Born in Milwaukee. He earned his PhD at Harvard University. He is a fellow at the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and a professor at the University of Colorado Boulder. His group first demonstrated a quantum logic gate with two ions and created a 'Schrödinger cat' state from several ions.
key_discoveries: Laser cooling of ions to the ground state — achieving the minimum possible vibrational energy.
Creation of quantum gates based on trapped ions — one of the first working prototypes of a quantum computer.
Schrödinger cat states — superpositions of macroscopically distinguishable states of an ion ensemble.
David Wineland.quote: A quantum computer is not just a faster classical one; it operates differently, using superpositions and entanglement.
David Wineland.fun_fact: To cool ions to temperatures of a few microkelvin, his team uses lasers that reduce motion to the lowest energy state, similar to how a magnetic field slows down a pendulum.

Nicolaus Copernicus.name: Nicolaus Copernicus
Nicolaus Copernicus.description: Polish astronomer, mathematician, economist and canon, author of the heliocentric system of the world, which initiated the scientific revolution of the 16th century. He placed the Sun at the center of the planetary system and made the Earth one of the planets, thus destroying Ptolemy's geocentric model. His work "On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres" became a turning point in the history of science.
Nicolaus Copernicus.biography: Born in Toruń. He studied at the University of Krakow, then in Italy (Bologna, Padua, Ferrara), where he studied law, medicine and astronomy. After returning, he worked as a canon and administrator in Frombork, simultaneously engaging in astronomical observations and mathematical calculations.
key_discoveries: Heliocentric system — proposed a model in which the Earth and other planets revolve around a stationary Sun, explaining the diurnal and annual motions of celestial bodies.
Explanation of retrograde motion of planets — showed that the apparent retrograde motion of planets is a consequence of the Earth's motion, not a real deceleration.
Calendar reform — participated in the preparation of the Gregorian calendar reform.
Copernicus' economic law — formulated the principle later known as Gresham's law: "bad money drives out good".
Nicolaus Copernicus.quote: "The Sun sits on a throne, governing the family of planets revolving around it."
Nicolaus Copernicus.fun_fact: Copernicus was a versatile person: he served as a physician, managed the economic affairs of the diocese, developed dam projects, and even wrote poetry.

J. J. Thomson.name: J. J. Thomson
J. J. Thomson.description: English physicist who discovered the electron, the first elementary particle. He proved that cathode rays consist of negatively charged particles and measured their charge-to-mass ratio. He proposed one of the first atomic models (the "plum pudding" model). His work paved the way for electronics and atomic physics.
J. J. Thomson.biography: Born near Manchester. He studied at Owens College and the University of Cambridge (Trinity College). Professor at the Cavendish Laboratory, where he created a powerful school of experimental physicists. Nobel Prize in Physics laureate in 1906. His students included Rutherford, Bohr, Oppenheimer and others.
key_discoveries: Discovery of the electron (1897) — in experiments with cathode rays, he proved that they are a stream of particles, which he called electrons, and measured their charge-to-mass ratio.
Isotopes — discovered that the element neon consists of atoms with different masses, opening the existence of stable isotopes.
Thomson atomic model — represented the atom as a uniformly positively charged sphere with embedded electrons.
Leadership of the Cavendish Laboratory — transformed it into a world center of physics, which trained a generation of Nobel laureates.
J. J. Thomson.quote: "Physics is everything; everything else is stamp collecting" (paraphrasing Rutherford).
J. J. Thomson.fun_fact: J.J. Thomson's son, George Paget Thomson, also won a Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of electron diffraction.

Lise Meitner.name: Lise Meitner
Lise Meitner.description: Austrian physicist of Swedish descent, co-discoverer of nuclear fission of uranium. Together with Otto Hahn, she investigated nuclear reactions, but due to anti-Semitic persecution, she was forced to leave Germany. It was she, together with her nephew Otto Frisch, who gave the physical explanation of the fission process and estimated the energy released. Only Hahn received the Nobel Prize, which is considered a historical injustice.
Lise Meitner.biography: Born in Vienna. She studied at the University of Vienna (one of the first women to earn a doctorate). She worked at the University of Berlin with Otto Hahn, then in Sweden (Nobel Institute). She never participated in military developments. The 109th chemical element, meitnerium, is named in her honor.
key_discoveries: Discovery of nuclear fission — together with Hahn and Strassmann, she participated in experiments leading to the discovery, and gave the correct interpretation of the results as a splitting of the nucleus.
Explanation of the fission mechanism — used the liquid drop model of the nucleus to show how a uranium nucleus could fall apart into two fragments, and calculated the energy yield.
Auger effect — independently of Auger discovered radiationless transitions in atoms.
Discovery of protactinium — together with Hahn isolated element 91 (protactinium).
Lise Meitner.quote: "Science makes people selfless; it teaches them to seek truth for the sake of truth."
Lise Meitner.fun_fact: Meitner was one of the first women physicists with a doctorate in Germany. Friends called her the "atomic aunt".

Christiaan Huygens.name: Christiaan Huygens
Christiaan Huygens.description: Dutch mathematician, physicist and astronomer, one of the founders of theoretical mechanics and optics. He created the wave theory of light and explained double refraction. He invented the pendulum clock and constructed an improved telescope, with which he discovered Saturn's rings and its moon Titan. He contributed to probability theory and mathematical physics.
Christiaan Huygens.biography: Born in The Hague. He studied at Leiden University and Breda University. He worked at the Paris Academy of Sciences, where he enjoyed the patronage of Louis XIV. He returned to Holland at the end of his life. He maintained extensive correspondence with contemporaries, including Newton and Leibniz.
key_discoveries: Wave theory of light — proposed that light propagates as waves, and successfully explained reflection, refraction, and double refraction in Iceland spar.
Invention of the pendulum clock — first applied a pendulum to regulate the movement of a clock, significantly increasing its accuracy, and received a patent.
Discovery of Saturn's rings and Titan — using a telescope he made himself, he correctly described the rings as a system of solid particles and discovered Saturn's largest moon.
Theory of centrifugal force — derived the formula for centrifugal acceleration and investigated the motion of a pendulum along a cycloid.
Huygens' principle — each element of a wavefront is a source of secondary waves, which underlies modern wave optics.
Christiaan Huygens.quote: "The world is my country, science is my religion."
Christiaan Huygens.fun_fact: Huygens was not only a scientist but also a musician; he invented a clavichord with 31 keys per octave for playing "pure" musical intervals.

William Herschel.name: William Herschel
William Herschel.description: German-British astronomer and composer, discoverer of the planet Uranus, as well as infrared radiation. He conducted a systematic survey of the sky, compiled catalogs of thousands of nebulae and star clusters. He built the largest reflecting telescopes of his time, which advanced the study of the structure of the Milky Way.
William Herschel.biography: Born in Hanover. He moved to England, first working as a musician (organist, conductor). He became interested in astronomy, grinding mirrors for telescopes himself, and became the King's Astronomer. His sister Caroline Herschel was a faithful assistant and herself became a renowned astronomer.
key_discoveries: Discovery of Uranus (1781) — for the first time since antiquity, discovered a new planet, doubling the boundaries of the solar system.
Infrared radiation — by measuring the temperature of different parts of the solar spectrum beyond the red limit, he discovered invisible "heat rays", later called infrared.
Surveys of double stars — proved that many of them are physically bound pairs, and began measurements of their orbits.
Structure of the Milky Way — proposed a model of our Galaxy as a disk, in which the Sun is not at the center.
Deep sky catalogs — together with his sister compiled the largest catalogs of nebulae and star clusters, many of which are known today as Herschel objects.
William Herschel.quote: "We have seen what was invisible, and have looked into the far corners of the Universe."
William Herschel.fun_fact: Herschel was a composer and wrote 24 symphonies, and built his first telescope from scrap materials, including a manure fork as a holder.

Albert Einstein.name: Albert Einstein
Albert Einstein.description: Theoretical physicist who forever changed our understanding of space, time and gravity. Creator of special and general relativity, explained the photoelectric effect, introduced the concept of the photon. His equation E=mc² became a symbol of a new era in physics. His contributions to quantum theory and statistical mechanics are also fundamental.
Albert Einstein.biography: Born in Ulm, Germany. Studied at the Swiss Polytechnic School in Zurich. Worked at the patent office in Bern, then professor in Zurich, Prague, Berlin. In the summer of 1933 he emigrated to the USA, worked at the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton. Winner of the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921 for his explanation of the photoelectric effect.
key_discoveries: Special theory of relativity (1905) — showed that time and space are relative and the speed of light is constant; introduced the concept of spacetime.
General theory of relativity (1915) — described gravity as the curvature of spacetime by matter and energy, predicted black holes and gravitational waves.
Photoelectric effect — explained the ejection of electrons by light, assuming the quantum nature of light (photons), which was a key step in the development of quantum mechanics.
Quantum theory of heat capacity — applied the quantum hypothesis to explain the heat capacity of solids at low temperatures.
Albert Einstein.quote: “Imagination is more important than knowledge, for knowledge is limited.”
Albert Einstein.fun_fact: As a child, Einstein started speaking so late that his parents feared he might be developmentally delayed. And later, he never learned to drive a car.

Isaac Newton.name: Isaac Newton
Isaac Newton.description: English mathematician, physicist, astronomer, and alchemist, one of the most influential scientists in history. He formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation, laying the foundations of classical mechanics. He made enormous contributions to optics, proving that white light consists of a spectrum of colors. He developed differential and integral calculus simultaneously with Leibniz.
Isaac Newton.biography: Born in Woolsthorpe, England. Graduated from Cambridge University (Trinity College). Professor of mathematics at Cambridge, later president of the Royal Society. Author of “Mathematical Principles of Natural Philosophy” and “Optics”. Buried in Westminster Abbey.
key_discoveries: Law of universal gravitation — explained that the gravitational force between bodies is proportional to their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance; united celestial and terrestrial mechanics.
Three laws of motion — the basis of classical mechanics, describing the behavior of bodies under forces (inertia, acceleration, action-reaction).
Discovery of the spectrum of white light — using a prism, he decomposed white light into rainbow colors and proved that this is a property of light itself, not of the prism.
Invention of the reflecting telescope — built the first functional reflector telescope, free from chromatic aberration.
Mathematical analysis — independently of Leibniz, developed differential and integral calculus.
Isaac Newton.quote: “If I have seen further, it is by standing on the shoulders of giants.”
Isaac Newton.fun_fact: Newton was a passionate alchemist and spent decades searching for the philosopher's stone, which was long concealed to avoid damaging his reputation.

Werner Heisenberg.name: Werner Heisenberg
Werner Heisenberg.description: German theoretical physicist, one of the creators of quantum mechanics. He formulated the matrix version of quantum theory and the famous uncertainty principle, which states that it is impossible to simultaneously measure the exact position and momentum of a particle. He contributed to nuclear theory, quantum electrodynamics, and particle physics.
Werner Heisenberg.biography: Born in Würzburg. Studied at the University of Munich, worked with Niels Bohr, Max Born, Pascual Jordan. Professor in Leipzig, director of the Max Planck Institute for Physics. Winner of the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1932 for the creation of quantum mechanics.
key_discoveries: Matrix mechanics (1925) — the first consistent mathematical formalism of quantum mechanics, describing physical quantities as matrices.
Uncertainty principle (1927) — established a fundamental limit to the precision of simultaneous measurement of certain pairs of variables, such as position and momentum.
Isospin — introduced the concept of isotopic spin to explain the symmetry of the proton and neutron in nuclear interactions.
Model of ferromagnetism — explained the nature of ferromagnetism through the exchange interaction of electrons.
Werner Heisenberg.quote: “The first gulp from the goblet of natural science makes you an atheist, but at the bottom of the goblet God awaits us.”
Werner Heisenberg.fun_fact: Heisenberg was a talented pianist and seriously agonized between a career as a musician and a physicist.

Max Planck.name: Max Planck
Max Planck.description: German theoretical physicist, founder of quantum theory, which revolutionized physics in the early 20th century. He introduced the concept of the energy quantum, explaining the radiation spectrum of a black body. Planck's constant became one of the fundamental constants of nature. His work spurred the creation of quantum mechanics.
Max Planck.biography: Born in Kiel. Studied at the Universities of Munich and Berlin, student of Helmholtz and Kirchhoff. Professor at the University of Berlin, president of the Kaiser Wilhelm Society (later renamed the Max Planck Society). Winner of the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1918 for the discovery of energy quanta.
key_discoveries: Quantum hypothesis (1900) — proposed that electromagnetic energy is emitted not continuously, but in discrete portions (quanta), solving the ultraviolet catastrophe problem.
Planck's constant — a fundamental constant linking the energy of a quantum to the frequency of radiation (E = hν).
Planck's law of radiation — accurately describes the radiation spectrum of a black body at all wavelengths.
Theory of heat capacity of solids — applied the quantum hypothesis to explain the behavior of heat capacity at low temperatures.
Max Planck.quote: “A new truth prevails not because its opponents are convinced, but because they eventually die out.”
Max Planck.fun_fact: Planck was a brilliant pianist and even considered becoming a professional musician, but chose physics. His son Erwin was executed by the Nazis for participating in a plot against Hitler.

Marie Curie.name: Marie Curie
Marie Curie.description: French physicist and chemist of Polish origin, pioneer in the study of radioactivity. She discovered the elements polonium and radium, developed methods for isolating radioactive isotopes. Twice winner of the Nobel Prize: in Physics (1903) and in Chemistry (1911). Her work laid the foundations of nuclear physics and medical radiology.
Marie Curie.biography: Born in Warsaw (then Russian Empire) as Maria Skłodowska. Studied at the Sorbonne in Paris. Married Pierre Curie, with whom she worked. After his death, she headed the laboratory, became the first female professor at the Sorbonne. She founded the Radium Institute in Paris.
key_discoveries: Discovery of radioactivity — together with Pierre Curie, she investigated uranium radiation and coined the term 'radioactivity'.
Discovery of polonium and radium — isolated from uranium ore two new chemical elements, significantly more active than uranium.
Methods of isotope separation — developed laborious chemical procedures to extract pure radioactive substances from tons of ore.
Medical radiology — promoted the use of radioactive radiation for the diagnosis and treatment of cancer.
Marie Curie.quote: “Nothing in life is to be feared, it is only to be understood. Now is the time to understand more, so that we may fear less.”
Marie Curie.fun_fact: Maria Curie used her Nobel medals as toys for her daughter Irène, who later also won a Nobel Prize in Chemistry.

Ernest Rutherford.name: Ernest Rutherford
Ernest Rutherford.description: British physicist of New Zealand origin, the 'father of nuclear physics'. He discovered the atomic nucleus in the famous alpha-particle scattering experiment. He explained the nature of radioactivity, discovered the proton, and proposed the nuclear model of the atom. He was the first to achieve artificial transmutation of elements (transmutation).
Ernest Rutherford.biography: Born in New Zealand. Studied at Canterbury College, then at the Cavendish Laboratory in Cambridge under J.J. Thomson. Professor at the University of Manchester and University of Cambridge. Winner of the 1908 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for his research on radioactivity.
key_discoveries: Discovery of the atomic nucleus (1911) — experiments on scattering of alpha particles by gold foil showed that almost all the mass of the atom is concentrated in a tiny positively charged nucleus.
Rutherford model of the atom — proposed a planetary model in which electrons revolve around the nucleus.
Discovery of the proton (1919) — identified the hydrogen nucleus as a fundamental particle, the proton.
Artificial transmutation — for the first time transformed nitrogen into oxygen by bombarding it with alpha particles, proving the possibility of changing atomic nuclei.
Ernest Rutherford.quote: All science is either physics or stamp collecting.
Ernest Rutherford.fun_fact: When Rutherford learned that he had received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry rather than Physics, he joked that it was the only time he had managed to turn from a physicist into a chemist.

Michael Faraday.name: Michael Faraday
Michael Faraday.description: English physicist and chemist, one of the greatest experimentalists in the history of science. He made fundamental contributions to the theory of electromagnetism: discovered electromagnetic induction, the laws of electrolysis, the rotation of the plane of polarization of light in a magnetic field. Invented the first electric motor and dynamo. His concept of the field became dominant in physics.
Michael Faraday.biography: Born in London to a poor family, had almost no formal education. Worked as a bookbinder, then as a laboratory assistant to Humphry Davy. Became a professor at the Royal Institution, where he delivered the famous Christmas lectures. Declined many honors, including the presidency of the Royal Society.
key_discoveries: Electromagnetic induction — showed that a changing magnetic field generates an electric current in a conductor, which became the basis of electric power generation.
Laws of electrolysis — established a quantitative relationship between the amount of electricity passed and the mass of substance deposited.
Faraday effect — discovered the rotation of the plane of polarization of light under the influence of a magnetic field, proving the connection between light and magnetism.
Invention of the electric motor — created the first primitive motor converting electrical energy into mechanical energy.
Concept of the field — proposed the idea of lines of force and fields to describe electrical and magnetic interactions.
Michael Faraday.quote: Nothing is too wonderful to be true, if it be consistent with the laws of nature.
Michael Faraday.fun_fact: Faraday was a deeply religious Christian and attended church every Sunday. Due to poor health, he declined offers to be buried in Westminster Abbey next to Newton.

Enrico Fermi.name: Enrico Fermi
Enrico Fermi.description: Italian-American physicist, the 'architect of the nuclear age'. He built the first nuclear reactor, thus initiating controlled nuclear chain reaction. Developed Fermi-Dirac statistics for particles with half-integer spin. Made fundamental contributions to the theory of beta decay, introduced the concept of the weak interaction. Exceptionally successfully combined theoretical and experimental work.
Enrico Fermi.biography: Born in Rome. Studied at the University of Pisa. Worked in Göttingen and Leiden, then professor at the University of Rome. In 1938, due to anti-Semitic laws, emigrated to the USA, worked at Columbia University and the University of Chicago. Winner of the 1938 Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of new radioactive elements.
key_discoveries: Theory of beta decay — explained the emission of an electron from the nucleus, introducing a new particle, the neutrino, and described the weak interaction.
Fermi-Dirac statistics — developed a statistical description of systems of particles with half-integer spin (fermions) obeying the Pauli exclusion principle.
First nuclear reactor (Chicago Pile-1, 1942) — built and commissioned the world's first nuclear reactor, demonstrating a controlled chain reaction.

Discovery of transuranium elements — neutron bombardment of uranium led to the creation of new elements, for which he received the Nobel Prize (later it turned out that nuclear fission had been observed).
Enrico Fermi.quote: Where is everybody? (Fermi paradox about extraterrestrial intelligence).
Enrico Fermi.fun_fact: Fermi was known for his ability to quickly make accurate estimates, called 'Fermi problems' — for example, estimating the energy of a nuclear explosion by eye from the movement of pieces of paper.

Galileo Galilei.name: Galileo Galilei
Galileo Galilei.description: Italian physicist, astronomer, and mathematician, the 'father of modern science'. He was the first to systematically use the experimental method and mathematical description in natural science. He improved the telescope and made revolutionary astronomical discoveries, confirming the Copernican heliocentric system. He formulated the principle of relativity and the law of inertia, laying the foundations of classical mechanics.
Galileo Galilei.biography: Born in Pisa. Studied at the University of Pisa, then taught mathematics in Padua and later was court mathematician in Florence. For supporting heliocentrism, he was tried by the Inquisition and forced to recant. Author of 'Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems'.
key_discoveries: Astronomical discoveries with the telescope — discovered mountains on the Moon, sunspots, the four largest moons of Jupiter (Galilean moons), the phases of Venus, which proved its rotation around the Sun.
Law of falling bodies — showed that in the absence of air resistance, bodies fall with the same acceleration regardless of mass.
Principle of relativity (Galilean transformations) — stated that the laws of mechanics are the same in all inertial frames of reference.
Invention of the thermoscope — a precursor to the thermometer.
Theory of tides — attempted (incorrectly) to explain tides by the motion of the Earth.
Galileo Galilei.fun_fact: Galileo was a skilled lute player and passed his love of music to his son, who became a composer.

Johannes Kepler.name: Johannes Kepler
Johannes Kepler.description: German astronomer, mathematician, and optician, who discovered the laws of planetary motion named after him. He showed that planets move in elliptical orbits with variable speed, and established a mathematical relationship between the orbital period and the distance from the Sun. His work became the foundation for Newton's theory of gravity. He also contributed to optics and stereometry.
Johannes Kepler.biography: Born in Weil der Stadt. Studied at the University of Tübingen, where he became a supporter of Copernicus' teachings. Worked as an assistant to Tycho Brahe, after his death inherited the most precise astronomical observations. Imperial mathematician in Prague under Rudolf II. Author of books 'New Astronomy', 'Harmonices Mundi'.
key_discoveries: Three laws of planetary motion (Kepler's laws): 1) planets move in ellipses with the Sun at one focus; 2) in equal intervals of time, the radius vector sweeps out equal areas (law of areas); 3) the squares of the orbital periods of planets are proportional to the cubes of the semi-major axes of their orbits.
Optical theory — explained the functioning of the human eye and described the refraction of light in lenses.
Kepler's Supernova — observed and described the outburst of a supernova, proving that changes occur even in the realm of the 'eternal' stars.
Keplerian telescope — proposed a telescope design with two convex lenses.
Johannes Kepler.quote: 'I wrote a book so that it would be read by contemporaries or descendants — it doesn't matter. It can wait hundreds of years for its reader, if God himself waited six thousand years to see his spectator.'
Johannes Kepler.fun_fact: Kepler believed in the 'music of the spheres' and sought harmonic relationships between the orbits of the planets, trying to fit them into a system of polyhedra.

Artur Ekert.name: Artur Ekert
Artur Ekert.description: British-Polish physicist, one of the founders of quantum cryptography. Developed the E91 (Ekert) protocol, using entangled photon pairs for secure key distribution. His work combined Bell's concepts and quantum information, proving that quantum mechanics can guarantee security.
Artur Ekert.biography: Studied physics at Jagiellonian University and Oxford, where he received his PhD. Worked at Oxford, then founded the Centre for Quantum Technologies in Singapore. Laureate of the Descartes Prize and the Hughes Medal. Professor at Oxford University and visiting professor in Singapore.
key_discoveries: E91 protocol: quantum key distribution based on violation of Bell inequalities, providing provable security independent of device trust.
Contributions to quantum computing theory: showed that any unitary operations are realizable in a quantum system.
Research in quantum machine learning and quantum networks.
Artur Ekert.quote: Quantum cryptography is the first technology that gives us absolute security based on fundamental laws of nature.
Artur Ekert.fun_fact: Ekert is an avid pilot and flies his own plane; he jokes that flying a plane is easier than operating a quantum computer.

Gilles Brassard.name: Gilles Brassard
Gilles Brassard.description: Canadian scientist in quantum information. One of the inventors of quantum cryptography — together with Charles Bennett proposed the BB84 protocol, which became the first practical method of quantum key distribution. He also made fundamental contributions to quantum teleportation, quantum error correction, and other areas.
Gilles Brassard.biography: Received his PhD from the University of Montreal, where he teaches. Fellow of the Royal Society of Canada and the Royal Society of London. Awarded the Wolf Prize in Physics and other honors for creating the foundations of quantum information science.
key_discoveries: BB84 protocol: the first quantum cryptography protocol, using four polarization states of photons for secure key transmission.
Quantum teleportation: proposed the first scheme for teleporting a quantum state using entanglement and classical communication.
Quantum error correction and distributed quantum computing.
Gilles Brassard.quote: We realized that the laws of quantum mechanics are not an obstacle to secure communication, but its most reliable ally.
Gilles Brassard.fun_fact: Brassard calls himself a "zeroth-generation quantum programmer" and regrets that he won't live to see the day when quantum computers become commonplace.

David DiVincenzo.name: David DiVincenzo
David DiVincenzo.description: American physicist, known for formulating the DiVincenzo criteria — a list of necessary conditions for the physical realization of a quantum computer. These criteria have become a benchmark for quantum computing system developers worldwide.
David DiVincenzo.biography: Received his PhD from the University of Pennsylvania, worked at IBM Thomas J. Watson Research Center and as a visiting scientist at various institutes. Currently a professor at RWTH Aachen University and scientific head of quantum computing at Forschungszentrum Jülich.
key_discoveries: DiVincenzo criteria: a set of five (later seven) requirements that a physical chip must satisfy to implement quantum computing: scalable qubits, initialization, long coherence time, a universal gate set, readout measurement, and others.
Research on spin qubits in silicon and superconducting qubits.
Work on quantum error correction and topological quantum computing.
David DiVincenzo.quote: The path to a quantum computer is not one big idea, but many small, yet correct steps.
David DiVincenzo.fun_fact: Initially, DiVincenzo formulated the criteria for internal use at IBM, but later they were published and changed the industry's approach to designing quantum processors.

Daniel Gottesman.name: Daniel Gottesman
Daniel Gottesman.description: American-Canadian physicist, specialist in quantum error correction. Developed the theory of stabilizer codes, which underlies most modern error correction schemes in quantum computers. His work helped establish precise thresholds of accuracy for quantum computing.
Daniel Gottesman.biography: Graduated from the California Institute of Technology (bachelor's) and received his PhD from Berkeley. Worked at Microsoft Research, MIT, and is now a professor at the University of Maryland. Elected Fellow of the American Physical Society.
key_discoveries: Stabilizer codes: a broad class of quantum codes, including Shor's code and CSS codes, described via Abelian groups, simplifying their analysis and implementation.
Threshold theorem for quantum computing: together with others showed that below a certain error rate, scalable quantum computation with correction is possible.
Research on the connection between quantum error correction and entanglement.
Daniel Gottesman.quote: The irony is that to protect quantum information from the outside world, we must actively interact with it, measuring errors.
Daniel Gottesman.fun_fact: Gottesman is also a well-known popularizer; his visualizations of stabilizer codes have helped many students understand abstract concepts.

Michael Berry.name: Michael Berry
Michael Berry.description: British theoretical physicist, known for discovering the Berry phase (geometric phase) in quantum mechanics. This phase, acquired by the wave function during adiabatic transport along a closed loop in parameter space, has profound implications in condensed matter physics, molecular physics, and even robotics.
Michael Berry.biography: Graduated from the University of Exeter and St Andrews, where he received his PhD. Spent his entire career at the University of Bristol. Awarded the Lorentz Medal, Max Planck Medal, and Wolf Prize. Knight bachelor since 1996. Known for elegant explanations of complex physical phenomena (e.g., rainbows and rogue waves).
key_discoveries: Berry phase: an additional phase factor acquired by the wave function under cyclic parameter variation, depending on path geometry, not time.
Connection of the Berry phase with the Aharonov–Bohm effect and with light polarization in optical fibers.
Application of geometric phases to understand the nature of 'rogue waves' and caustics.
Michael Berry.quote: The most beautiful physics is often hidden behind simple, everyday phenomena — like rainbows or reflections on water.
Michael Berry.fun_fact: Berry was a scientific consultant for the film "District 9" and helped the director devise physically plausible alien technologies.

Roger Penrose.name: Roger Penrose
Roger Penrose.description: British physicist and mathematician, made fundamental contributions to general relativity and cosmology. Proved that the formation of black holes is an inevitable consequence of general relativity under realistic conditions. Developed twistor theory, which aims to unify quantum mechanics and gravity. Nobel Prize laureate in Physics for discoveries in the field of black holes.
Roger Penrose.biography: Graduated from University College London and the University of Cambridge. Worked at Birkbeck College, University of Oxford. Collaborated with Stephen Hawking on singularity theorems. Received numerous awards, including the Nobel Prize (2020), the Wolf Prize, and the Copley Medal.
key_discoveries: Singularity theorems: jointly with Hawking showed that within the framework of general relativity there must be a Big Bang singularity in the past, and black holes contain singularities.
Penrose process: a mechanism for extracting energy from a rotating black hole.
Conformal cyclic cosmology: a model of the universe in which the end of a previous aeon smoothly transitions into the beginning of a new one.
Roger Penrose.quote: Consciousness is a phenomenon that cannot be explained solely by computation. There is something elusive about it.
Roger Penrose.fun_fact: Penrose is known for his impossible figures, such as the Penrose triangle, which inspire artists and illustrate visual paradoxes.

Max Born.name: Max Born
Max Born.description: German and British theoretical physicist, one of the founders of quantum mechanics. Introduced the probabilistic interpretation of the wave function (Born rule), for which he received the Nobel Prize. Contributed to solid-state physics, optics, and fluid physics.
Max Born.biography: Studied in Breslau, Heidelberg, Zurich, and Göttingen. Received his doctorate from the University of Göttingen. Worked in Berlin, Frankfurt, Göttingen, and after the rise of the Nazis, in Cambridge and Edinburgh. Teacher of many famous physicists, including Oppenheimer and Teller. Nobel Prize (1954).
key_discoveries: Probabilistic interpretation of the wave function: the square of the modulus of the wave function gives the probability density of finding a particle, which laid the foundation for the modern understanding of quantum measurements.
Born-Oppenheimer approximation: separation of nuclear and electronic motion in molecules, a fundamental method in quantum chemistry.
Max Born.quote: The idea that an electron freely chooses the moment and direction of its jump is unbearable to me. If so, I would rather be a shoemaker or even a casino employee than a physicist.
Max Born.fun_fact: Born was a good pianist and often played chamber music with colleagues; his daughter Olivia became a well-known poet, and his granddaughter became the actress and singer Olivia Newton-John.

Eugene Wigner.name: Eugene Wigner
Eugene Wigner.description: Hungarian-American theoretical physicist, one of the pioneers of quantum mechanics and nuclear physics. Introduced symmetries into quantum theory, developed the theory of group representations in physics. Described the 'Wigner's friend' paradox, concerning the role of the observer. Nobel Prize laureate.
Eugene Wigner.biography: Studied at the Berlin Institute of Technology, where he earned a degree in chemical engineering, but became interested in physics. Worked in Göttingen (assistant to Hilbert), Princeton, participated in the Manhattan Project. Remained a professor at Princeton University until retirement. Nobel Prize (1963).
key_discoveries: Application of group theory to quantum mechanics: explained atomic spectra and properties of elementary particles through symmetries.
Wigner's friend paradox: a thought experiment raising the measurement problem in quantum mechanics without wave function collapse.
Wigner-Eckart theorem: relation between matrix elements of operators and the symmetry of the system.
Eugene Wigner.quote: The miraculous puzzle of the correspondence between the mathematical language and the laws of physics is a wonderful gift that we neither understand nor deserve.
Eugene Wigner.fun_fact: As a teenager, Wigner attended the same gymnasium as John von Neumann; their friendship led to a long collaboration and mutual influence.

Hugh Everett III.name: Hugh Everett III
Hugh Everett III.description: American physicist, creator of the many-worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics (1957), which asserts that the wave function never collapses, and all possible outcomes of quantum events are realized in parallel universes. Although his ideas were not recognized during his lifetime, they later became the foundation of quantum cosmology and decoherence.
Hugh Everett III.biography: Graduated from the Catholic University of America, then studied physics at Princeton under John Wheeler. His dissertation 'The Theory of the Universal Wave Function' contained the many-worlds interpretation. After academic setbacks, worked in the defense sector, doing mathematical modeling and analysis.
key_discoveries: Many-worlds interpretation: rejection of wave function collapse and postulation of the existence of many classical worlds branching off at every quantum event.
Theory of the universal wave function: formalism in which the observer and the measured system are described by a single quantum wave function.
Hugh Everett III.quote: The objective world simply exists, and there is no need for wave function collapse.
Hugh Everett III.fun_fact: Everett was a passionate proponent of rational egoism and believed that the many-worlds interpretation guaranteed him immortality, as there would always be a branch where he survives.

Juan Maldacena.name: Juan Maldacena
Juan Maldacena.description: Argentine theoretical physicist, who proposed the AdS/CFT correspondence (Maldacena conjecture), which links string theory in anti-de Sitter space with conformal field theory on its boundary. This revolutionary discovery made it possible to study strongly interacting quantum systems through gravity and vice versa.
Juan Maldacena.biography: Received his PhD from Princeton University in 1996. Worked at Harvard, and since 2001 has been a professor at the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton. Recipient of numerous awards, including the Lorentz Medal, the Gruber Prize in Cosmology, and the Fundamental Physics Prize.
key_discoveries: AdS/CFT correspondence: duality between quantum gravity in AdS space and conformal field theory on the boundary, providing a powerful tool for studying quantum gravity and strong interactions.
Research on quantum black holes: clarified the connection between black hole entropy and entanglement in the dual theory.
Juan Maldacena.quote: The universe is arranged in such a way that the apparent complexity of a black hole can be described by a simple theory on the boundary.
Juan Maldacena.fun_fact: Maldacena is one of the most cited physicists of today; his 1998 paper on AdS/CFT has accumulated tens of thousands of citations.

Edward Witten.name: Edward Witten
Edward Witten.description: American theoretical physicist, one of the leading experts in string theory and quantum field theory. Made fundamental contributions to the unification of various string theories within M-theory, as well as to topological quantum field theory. The only physicist to have been awarded the Fields Medal.
Edward Witten.biography: Studied history and economics, then switched to physics and received his PhD from Princeton. Became a professor at Princeton, and since 1987 has worked at the Institute for Advanced Study. Winner of the Fields Medal (1990), the Gruber Prize, the U.S. National Medal of Science and many other awards.
key_discoveries: M-theory: showed that the five known string theories are related by dualities and are special cases of a unified 11-dimensional theory.
Topological quantum field theory: applied topology methods to compute invariants in quantum theory, for example, the Jones polynomial in knot theory.
Connections between supersymmetric field theories and geometry.
Edward Witten.quote: String theory is a part of 21st-century physics that fell by chance into the 20th century.
Edward Witten.fun_fact: Witten is the son of theoretical physicist Louis Witten; his father joked that he was the most famous Witten until Edward won the Fields Medal.

Alexei Kitaev.name: Alexei Kitaev
Alexei Kitaev.description: Russian-American physicist known for his work in quantum computing. He proposed the concept of topological quantum computing based on anyons and developed the quantum phase estimation algorithm. He introduced the Kitaev chain, a model that demonstrates Majorana fermions, which are important for protected qubits.
Alexei Kitaev.biography: Graduated from the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology and the Landau Institute for Theoretical Physics. Worked at Microsoft Research and the California Institute of Technology. Received a MacArthur Fellowship, the Dirac Medal, and the Fundamental Physics Prize.
key_discoveries: Topological quantum computing: the idea of using anyons for error-resistant quantum computation.
Kitaev's quantum phase estimation algorithm: an efficient method for problems that reduce to finding eigenvalues.
Kitaev chain model: a system in which Majorana modes emerge, promising for the creation of topological qubits.
Alexei Kitaev.quote: Topological quantum computing could be the path to creating truly scalable quantum computers.
Alexei Kitaev.fun_fact: Kitaev is known for his distinctive presentation style — his lectures and papers often contain unexpected insights that later become standard approaches.

Peter Shor.name: Peter Shor
Peter Shor.description: American mathematician and quantum computing specialist. In 1994, he proposed the famous Shor's algorithm for factoring integers on a quantum computer in polynomial time, which demonstrated a practical threat to classical cryptography and spurred the development of quantum technologies.
Peter Shor.biography: Received a bachelor's degree in mathematics from Caltech and a PhD from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. Works at MIT, where he is a professor of applied mathematics. Winner of the Gödel Prize, the Dirac Medal, and the Breakthrough Prize in Fundamental Physics.
key_discoveries: Shor's algorithm: a quantum algorithm that efficiently factors numbers, breaking RSA and other cryptosystems.
Quantum error correction: one of the first to propose methods for protecting quantum information from noise.
Work on the limits of quantum computation.
Peter Shor.quote: Quantum computers use fundamentally different resources than classical ones, and this gives them incredible power.
Peter Shor.fun_fact: Shor enjoys juggling and sometimes performs at scientific conferences, demonstrating connections between mathematics and circus arts.

Lov Grover.name: Lov Grover
Lov Grover.description: Indian-American scientist in the field of quantum computing. In 1996, he developed Grover's algorithm for searching an unstructured database with a quadratic speedup compared to classical methods. His work demonstrated the universal utility of quantum algorithms.
Lov Grover.biography: Received a PhD in electrical engineering from Stanford University. Worked at Bell Labs, then became an independent researcher. Recipient of the Dirac Medal and other awards for contributions to quantum information science.
key_discoveries: Grover's algorithm: a quantum search algorithm that finds a target element in an unsorted set of N items in about √N steps, providing a quadratic speedup.
Research on amplitude amplification, a generalization of Grover's algorithm to a wider class of problems.
Lov Grover.quote: Quantum computing is not just faster — it uses completely different laws of physics to process information.
Lov Grover.fun_fact: Grover earned a degree in electrical engineering, but his true passion has always been physics; the algorithm came to him while walking on the beach.

John Preskill.name: John Preskill
John Preskill.description: American theoretical physicist, one of the pioneers of quantum information and quantum computing. He coined the term 'quantum supremacy' and described the stages of development of quantum technologies. Known for his work on topological quantum computing, quantum error correction, and the connection between black holes and quantum information.
John Preskill.biography: Studied at Princeton (undergraduate) and Harvard (PhD). Professor at the California Institute of Technology, where he directs the Institute for Quantum Information and Matter. Actively popularizes science, gives a famous lecture course and runs the blog 'Quantum Frontiers'.
key_discoveries: Concept of quantum supremacy: a stage at which a quantum computer performs a task beyond the reach of classical supercomputers — this idea became a driving force for the industry.
Topological quantum information: research on anyons and toric codes for creating error-resilient qubits.
Firewall paradox: participated in formulating the black hole firewall paradox concerning information preservation.
John Preskill.quote: We are at the dawn of the quantum era — a time when we are learning to control complex quantum systems, possibly the most important frontier of modern science.
John Preskill.fun_fact: Preskill is known for losing a bet with a colleague (Stephen Jordan) about the achievement of quantum supremacy, but actively promotes the event, hailing it as a great achievement.

Rolf Landauer.name: Rolf Landauer
Rolf Landauer.description: German-American physicist, pioneer in the field of information physics. Formulated Landauer's principle, linking the erasure of information with the inevitable release of heat, which became the foundation for the theory of reversible and quantum computing.
Rolf Landauer.biography: Born in Stuttgart. Emigrated to the USA in 1938. Studied at Harvard, received his PhD there. Spent his entire career at IBM Research, where he became one of the leading scientists. Member of the National Academy of Sciences and the National Academy of Engineering. Recipient of numerous awards, including the Oliver E. Buckley Prize.
key_discoveries: Landauer's principle (1961)
according to which erasing one bit of information at temperature T requires a minimum energy of kT ln 2
the idea of reversible logic and the connection between information and thermodynamics
contribution to the understanding of conductivity in mesoscopic systems.
Rolf Landauer.fun_fact: Landauer was a well-known skeptic regarding the exponential growth of computing power, which led to heated debates with colleagues.

Asher Peres.name: Asher Peres
Asher Peres.description: Israeli theoretical physicist, one of the founders of quantum information theory. Developed the Peres-Horodecki criterion for detecting entanglement. Made important contributions to quantum teleportation, quantum measurement, and the foundations of quantum mechanics.
Asher Peres.biography: Born in France. Studied at the Technion (Haifa), where he worked as a professor all his life. Was a close colleague of David Bohm and Charles Bennett. Wrote the classic textbook 'Quantum Theory: Concepts and Methods'. Member of the New York Academy of Sciences.
key_discoveries: Peres criterion (1996)
which became the basis for measuring entanglement
contributions to quantum teleportation and quantum computing
research on quantum chaos and measurements.
Asher Peres.quote: Quantum mechanics is not a theory about what we don't know; it's a theory about what we cannot know.
Asher Peres.fun_fact: Peres was known for his wit; once he wrote a humorous article about violating the uncertainty principle, which was accepted for publication.

Wojciech Zurek.name: Wojciech Zurek
Wojciech Zurek.description: Polish-American physicist, leading researcher in the field of quantum decoherence. Developed the concept of quantum Darwinism and the theory of the environment as the cause of the transition from quantum superposition to the classical world. Collaborated closely with John Wheeler.
Wojciech Zurek.biography: Born in Poland. Studied in Krakow, then in the USA, PhD at the University of Texas under the supervision of John Wheeler. Worked at Caltech and Los Alamos National Laboratory, then at the Santa Fe Institute. Member of the U.S. National Academy of Sciences.
key_discoveries: Theory of quantum decoherence (main works since the 1980s)
quantum Darwinism (2000s)
explaining
how classical reality is 'naturally selected' from the quantum world
contributions to quantum information and black hole physics.
Wojciech Zurek.quote: Decoherence is the mechanism by which the quantum world 'gives way' to the classical.
Wojciech Zurek.fun_fact: Zurek collaborated with Wheeler on the development of the concept 'it from bit', where information is fundamental.

Benjamin Schumacher.name: Benjamin Schumacher
Benjamin Schumacher.description: American physicist, introduced the term 'qubit' (quantum bit) and developed Schumacher's theorem on quantum data compression — a fundamental result of quantum information theory.
Benjamin Schumacher.biography: Born in the USA. Studied at the California Institute of Technology, received his PhD at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. Professor at Kenyon College (Ohio). Also known for science popularization.
key_discoveries: Concept of the qubit (1995)
Schumacher's theorem on quantum compression (analogue of Shannon's theorem for quantum channels)
contributions to quantum information theory and quantum error correction.
Benjamin Schumacher.quote: A qubit is a unit of quantum information that can store much more than a classical bit, thanks to superposition.
Benjamin Schumacher.fun_fact: The term 'qubit' arose as a play on words: from 'cubit' (an ancient measure of length of 45 cm) and 'quantum bit'.

William Wootters.name: William Wootters
William Wootters.description: American physicist, specialist in quantum information and entanglement. Together with Wojciech Zurek, proved the no-cloning theorem, a fundamental limitation of quantum mechanics. Developed a measure of entanglement — concurrence.
William Wootters.biography: Born in the USA. Studied at Stanford, received his PhD at the University of California, Berkeley. Worked at Williams College, where he taught until retirement. Collaborated closely with Charles Bennett and other quantum information pioneers.
key_discoveries: No-cloning theorem (1982
together with Zurek)
entanglement measure concurrence (1998)
research on quantum teleportation and quantum computing.
William Wootters.quote: You cannot create a perfect copy of an unknown quantum state — it is simply impossible.
William Wootters.fun_fact: In his free time, he enjoys the history of science and collects rare scientific books.

Gerard 't Hooft.name: Gerard 't Hooft
Gerard 't Hooft.description: Dutch theoretical physicist, one of the founders of modern quantum field theory. Made fundamental contributions to the proof of renormalizability of gauge theories, which laid the foundation for the Standard Model. Developed the holographic principle in quantum gravity, proposing a connection between quantum field theory and gravity.
Gerard 't Hooft.biography: Born in Den Helder. Studied at Utrecht University, where he obtained his doctorate under Martinus Veltman. Worked in Utrecht, then professor there. Winner of the Nobel Prize in Physics (1999) jointly with Veltman. Also known for work on instantons, magnetic monopoles, and quantum black holes.
key_discoveries: Proof of renormalizability of non-abelian gauge theories (with Veltman)
holographic principle (independently of Susskind)
contribution to the theory of quantum black holes
including S-matrix and holography.
Gerard 't Hooft.quote: I am convinced that the final theory must be beautiful and natural.
Gerard 't Hooft.fun_fact: In his youth, he was interested not only in physics but also in music, playing the saxophone. He is also known for his hobby of collecting scientific errors and misconceptions.

Carlo Rovelli.name: Carlo Rovelli
Carlo Rovelli.description: Italian theoretical physicist, one of the founders of loop quantum gravity. Made significant contributions to the development of a theory alternative to string theory, which describes the quantum structure of spacetime. Also known for philosophical works and science popularization.
Carlo Rovelli.biography: Born in Verona. Studied at the University of Bologna. Received his PhD in Padua. Worked at the University of Rome, University of Pittsburgh, and also in Marseille. Professor at the University of Marseille. Winner of numerous awards for popularization. Founder and active developer of loop quantum gravity together with Lee Smolin and Abhay Ashtekar.
key_discoveries: Loop quantum gravity (jointly with others)
theory of quantum spin networks
formulation of black hole thermodynamics in the framework of loop gravity.
Carlo Rovelli.quote: Reality is interaction. There are no objects outside of interactions; the world is a web of events.
