Carlo Rovelli.fun_fact: Passionate traveler and mountaineer; his love of mountains influenced his philosophical views on time and space. He also wrote about the nature of time in the bestseller 'The Order of Time'.

Bryce DeWitt.name: Bryce DeWitt
Bryce DeWitt.description: American theoretical physicist, one of the pioneers of quantum gravity. Together with John Wheeler, he derived the Wheeler-DeWitt equation—the fundamental equation of quantum cosmology. Made contributions to gauge theories, effective action, and quantum field theory in curved spacetime.
Bryce DeWitt.biography: Born in California. Studied at Harvard, received his PhD under Julian Schwinger. Worked at the Princeton Institute for Advanced Study, then at the University of North Carolina, and later at the University of Texas at Austin. Member of the National Academy of Sciences of the USA. Also known for work on the interpretation of quantum mechanics (he renamed Everett's many-worlds to 'many-worlds' and actively promoted it).
key_discoveries: Wheeler-DeWitt equation (1967)
background field method in quantum field theory
contributions to quantization of gravity and gauge theories.
Bryce DeWitt.quote: The laws of physics must be such that a physicist can discover them.
Bryce DeWitt.fun_fact: He and his wife Cécile DeWitt-Morette were a famous couple of physicists. Together they founded the summer school in theoretical physics in Les Houches, which became a breeding ground for Nobel laureates.

Yuri Manin.name: Yuri Manin
Yuri Manin.description: Soviet and Russian mathematician, one of the first to propose the concept of quantum computing. In 1980, he published the work 'Computable and Noncomputable', where he introduced the idea of a quantum automaton. Made fundamental contributions to algebraic geometry, number theory, and mathematical physics.
Yuri Manin.biography: Born in Simferopol. Studied at Moscow State University, where he later became a professor. Worked at the Steklov Mathematical Institute. From 1992 he worked at the Max Planck Institute for Mathematics in Bonn. Member of many academies, winner of the Lobachevsky Prize and other awards.
key_discoveries: First (along with independent works) mathematical justification of the possibility of quantum computers (1980)
contribution to algebraic geometry (theory of motives cohomology
Manin-Mumford conjecture)
theory of nonlinear differential equations
work on number theory.
Yuri Manin.quote: Proof is not just formal verification, it is explanation.
Yuri Manin.fun_fact: In addition to mathematics, he was interested in poetry and philosophy; he translated poetry from different languages and wrote essays on the relationship between mathematics and culture.

Paul Benioff.name: Paul Benioff
Paul Benioff.description: American theoretical physicist, the first to describe a quantum mechanical model of a computer. In 1980 (simultaneously with Yuri Manin) he showed that quantum systems can be used to perform computations, and introduced the concept of a quantum Turing machine.
Paul Benioff.biography: Born in New York. Educated in chemistry and physics, PhD in nuclear chemistry. Worked at Argonne National Laboratory. Despite modest fame, his work laid the foundation for quantum computing. He was active until the end of his life, publishing works on quantum information.
key_discoveries: Concept of a quantum computer as a quantum mechanical system
capable of simulating a classical Turing machine (1980)
development of a quantum Turing machine model
research on quantum algorithms and complexity.
Paul Benioff.quote: Quantum mechanics not only describes the world, but can also be used to process it.
Paul Benioff.fun_fact: He came to quantum computing from nuclear chemistry, which was atypical; his pioneering work long went unnoticed until the rise of interest in quantum computers.

Frank Wilczek.name: Frank Wilczek
Frank Wilczek.description: American theoretical physicist, Nobel laureate for the discovery of asymptotic freedom in quantum chromodynamics. Contributed to axion physics, anyons, and other ideas important for quantum computing.
Frank Wilczek.biography: Born in New York. Studied at the University of Chicago, PhD at Princeton under David Gross. Worked at the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton, then professor at MIT. Nobel laureate (2004) together with Gross and Politzer.
key_discoveries: Asymptotic freedom in QCD (1973)
prediction of anyons (particles with intermediate statistics
used in topological quantum computing)
theory of axion as a candidate for dark matter
concept of 'time crystals'.
Frank Wilczek.quote: Physics is the art of asking questions that nature answers with beautiful mathematics.
Frank Wilczek.fun_fact: Wilczek enjoys cooking and once compared the process of preparing an exotic dinner to creating a new theory.

John Bardeen.name: John Bardeen
John Bardeen.description: American physicist, the only person to receive two Nobel Prizes in physics. The first for the invention of the transistor (the foundation of modern electronics), the second for the BCS theory of superconductivity. His works underlie many quantum devices.
John Bardeen.biography: Born in Madison, Wisconsin. Studied at the University of Wisconsin, PhD at Princeton. Worked at Bell Labs, then professor at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign. Member of the National Academy of Sciences. Nobel laureate (1956, 1972).
key_discoveries: BCS theory of superconductivity (1957) (jointly with Cooper and Schrieffer)
explaining the quantum mechanism of superconductivity
transistor effect (1947) with Brattain and Shockley
surface states in semiconductors.
John Bardeen.quote: The main thing is to understand how little we actually know. That opens the door to discoveries.
John Bardeen.fun_fact: Bardeen was very modest and avoided the limelight; upon hearing of his second Nobel Prize, he first went to play golf.

Julian Schwinger.name: Julian Schwinger
Julian Schwinger.description: American theoretical physicist, one of the creators of quantum electrodynamics. Developed a powerful mathematical apparatus for quantum field theory, including the variational method and Green's function. His approaches formed the basis of many quantum computations.
Julian Schwinger.biography: Born in New York. A child prodigy, he defended his PhD at Columbia University at age 21. Worked at Purdue University, then at Harvard, and later at UCLA. Nobel laureate (1965) jointly with Feynman and Tomonaga for quantum electrodynamics.
key_discoveries: Mathematical formulation of quantum electrodynamics
eliminating divergences (independently of Feynman and Tomonaga)
Schwinger's proper time method
prediction of the existence of several types of neutrinos.
Julian Schwinger.quote: If you want to understand quantum mechanics, don't listen to what Bohr, Einstein, or anyone else says. Listen to the equations.
Julian Schwinger.fun_fact: Schwinger was known for his elegant formalism; colleagues joked that he 'hides simple ideas behind complex mathematics'.

Yakir Aharonov.name: Yakir Aharonov
Yakir Aharonov.description: Israeli physicist who predicted the Aharonov-Bohm effect, demonstrating the fundamental role of electromagnetic potentials in quantum mechanics even in the absence of fields. This effect has applications in quantum computing.
Yakir Aharonov.biography: Born in Haifa. Studied at the Technion, PhD at the University of Bristol (UK). Worked at the University of South Carolina, then at Tel Aviv and Chapman universities. Member of the US National Academy of Sciences and the Israel Academy of Sciences and Humanities. Recipient of many awards, including the Wolf Prize.
key_discoveries: Aharonov-Bohm effect (1959 with David Bohm)
showing
that the vector potential is physically significant
Aharonov-Bohm paradox
weak measurements and post-selection
expanding the concept of quantum measurement.
Yakir Aharonov.quote: Quantum mechanics teaches us that reality is richer than we could have imagined without equations.
Yakir Aharonov.fun_fact: Initially, colleagues were skeptical of the Aharonov-Bohm effect, but later it was confirmed experimentally and became a classic of quantum physics.

Leonard Susskind.name: Leonard Susskind
Leonard Susskind.description: American theoretical physicist, one of the founders of string theory. Made significant contributions to quantum theory of black holes, proposing the principle of complementarity and the holographic principle, resolving the information paradox.
Leonard Susskind.biography: Born in New York into a Jewish family. Initially studied to be a plumber, but then received an education at Columbia University. Worked at Stanford University, where he is a professor. Member of the US National Academy of Sciences. Also known as a science popularizer.
key_discoveries: Independent discovery of string theory (1970)
principle of black hole holography (black hole complementarity)
resolution of the information loss paradox
quantum field theory in curved spacetime
contributions to physics beyond the event horizon.
Leonard Susskind.quote: If you don't deeply understand quantum mechanics, you just haven't thought about it long enough.
Leonard Susskind.fun_fact: Susskind became famous for his 'battle with Hawking' over the issue of information preservation in black holes, which lasted for decades and ended with his viewpoint prevailing.

Ignacio Cirac.name: Ignacio Cirac
Ignacio Cirac.description: Spanish theoretical physicist, pioneer of quantum computing and quantum information. Together with Peter Zoller, he developed a scheme for a quantum computer based on trapped ions. He proposed the concepts of quantum repeaters for long-distance quantum communication and methods of quantum simulation. Director of the Max Planck Institute of Quantum Optics.
Ignacio Cirac.biography: He studied at the Complutense University of Madrid. He worked at the University of Castilla-La Mancha, the University of Innsbruck, and since 2001 has headed a department at the Max Planck Institute of Quantum Optics in Garching. Winner of the Wolf Prize (2013), the Prince of Asturias Award (2006), and many others.
key_discoveries: Ion trap quantum computer: together with Zoller, proposed a universal set of operations and a scalable architecture (1995).
Quantum repeaters: developed protocols to overcome losses and decoherence in optical channels, necessary for the quantum internet.
Tensor networks: contributed to methods for representing quantum states, efficient for simulating complex systems.
Ignacio Cirac.quote: "Quantum mechanics teaches us that information is physical, and we are only beginning to realize the consequences of this."
Ignacio Cirac.fun_fact: Cirac is known for his modesty: after receiving the Wolf Prize, he said that the main reward is the opportunity to work on what you love. He also actively popularizes science in Spanish-speaking countries.

Jeff Kimble.name: Jeff Kimble
Jeff Kimble.description: American experimental physicist, one of the leaders in quantum optics and quantum networks. Pioneer of cavity quantum electrodynamics (cavity QED), where atoms and photons interact under controlled conditions. His experiments on entangling distant atoms and creating quantum interfaces laid the foundations for the future quantum internet.
Jeff Kimble.biography: He graduated from the University of Rochester and received his PhD from the University of Michigan. He worked at the University of Texas and then at the California Institute of Technology (Caltech), where he headed the quantum optics laboratory. Member of the U.S. National Academy of Sciences. Winner of the Max Born Award and other honors.
key_discoveries: Cavity QED: realized the strong coupling regime between an atom and a photon in a cavity, enabling the exchange of quantum information (1990s).
Quantum networks: first demonstrated entanglement of two distant atoms via optical fiber (2007).
Squeezed light: developed sources of non-classical light with reduced noise for precision measurements.
Jeff Kimble.quote: "Light is not just a wave or a particle; it is a quantum entity that we are learning to tame bit by bit."
Jeff Kimble.fun_fact: Kimble's laboratory resembles a labyrinth of mirrors and lasers, where photons 'catch' atoms. His work is inspired by the idea of teleportation and quantum teleportation, first realized by his colleagues.

Philippe Grangier.name: Philippe Grangier
Philippe Grangier.description: French experimental physicist, pioneer of quantum cryptography and quantum optics. He was the first to experimentally demonstrate photon antibunching (1986), confirming the quantum nature of light. He made a decisive contribution to the creation of quantum key distribution systems based on weak laser pulses, which became a commercial technology.
Philippe Grangier.biography: He studied at the École Normale Supérieure and the University of Paris-Sud. He worked at the Institut d'Optique in Palaiseau, where he leads the quantum optics group. Winner of the Balzan Prize, the Albert Einstein Medal. Member of the French Academy of Sciences.
key_discoveries: Photon antibunching: first observed that a single atom emits photons one at a time, not in bunches, which became direct proof of the quantum nature of light (1986).
Plug-and-play quantum cryptography: developed a practical scheme for quantum key distribution with automatic compensation of polarization distortions, widely used in commercial systems.
Bell inequalities: conducted precision experiments closing loopholes in tests of local realism.
Philippe Grangier.quote: "The quantum world is not crazy; it is just much subtler than we are used to thinking."
Philippe Grangier.fun_fact: Grangier is a passionate popularizer: he created the world's first website on quantum cryptography and regularly gives lectures to schoolchildren. His work led to the creation of secure communication lines used by banks.

Daniel Greenberger.name: Daniel Greenberger
Daniel Greenberger.description: American theoretical physicist, known as one of the discoverers of the Greenberger–Horne–Zeilinger (GHZ) state — a multipartite entangled state that reveals radical contradictions between quantum mechanics and local realism without Bell inequalities. GHZ states have become a workhorse resource in quantum computing and communications.
Daniel Greenberger.biography: He studied at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, received his PhD from the University of Illinois. He worked at the University of California, Berkeley, and the City College of New York, where he chaired the department. Collaborated with Anton Zeilinger. Winner of the Majorana Prize.
key_discoveries: GHZ state: in 1989, together with Michael Horne and Anton Zeilinger, proposed a thought experiment with three or more particles, demonstrating the nonlocality of quantum mechanics in an "all or nothing" fashion.
Multipartite entanglement: explored the unique properties of GHZ states, which are used for quantum teleportation, superdense coding, and error correction.
Neutron interferometry: contributed to the quantum mechanics of neutrons, testing fundamental principles.
Daniel Greenberger.quote: "Quantum entanglement is not just a strangeness; it is the language in which Nature speaks to us about its wholeness."
Daniel Greenberger.fun_fact: Greenberger started as a neutron physicist and only later became interested in quantum paradoxes. The GHZ state is sometimes called "Einstein's nightmare," because it undermines classical concepts even more than the EPR paradox.

Michael Horne.name: Michael Horne
Michael Horne.description: American physicist, co-author of the GHZ state. He made significant contributions to quantum optics and quantum measurements, including the formulation of Bell's theorem with multiple observers and pioneering experiments on interference and entanglement.
Michael Horne.biography: He received his PhD from Boston University. He worked at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and Stonehill College, where he combined teaching with research. He worked closely with Anton Zeilinger and Daniel Greenberger.
key_discoveries: GHZ state: together with Greenberger and Zeilinger, proposed the state and proved its nonlocal properties (1989).
Multipartite Bell inequalities: developed generalizations of the inequalities for multiple observers, expanding the understanding of nonlocality.
Entangled-state interferometry: participated in experiments demonstrating quantum advantage in measurement precision.
Michael Horne.quote: "Reality is not made of parts until we ask it about that — and even then it surprises us."
Michael Horne.fun_fact: Horne is known for his wit: at conferences, he often illustrates quantum paradoxes with coin tricks. His co-author Zeilinger won the Nobel Prize for developing GHZ ideas.

Pascual Jordan.name: Pascual Jordan
Pascual Jordan.description: German theoretical physicist, one of the founders of quantum mechanics. He developed the matrix formulation of quantum mechanics simultaneously with Heisenberg and Born. He made fundamental contributions to quantum field theory, including the formalism of second quantization and the Jordan–Wigner transformation linking fermionic and spin systems. His works laid the foundations for modern quantum information and computation.
Pascual Jordan.biography: He studied at the University of Hanover, then at the University of Göttingen under Max Born. He worked at the University of Hamburg, the University of Rostock, and the University of Berlin. He collaborated with Werner Heisenberg and Wolfgang Pauli. Due to his membership in the Nazi party, his scientific legacy was long ignored. He was awarded the Max Planck Medal (1942). In the post-war years, he engaged in politics and the philosophy of science.
key_discoveries: Matrix mechanics: together with Born and Heisenberg formulated quantum mechanics in matrix form (1925).
Jordan algebra: introduced non-associative algebras for describing observables in quantum mechanics (1932).
Jordan–Wigner transformation: developed a method for mapping fermionic operators to spin operators, widely used in quantum simulations and computations (1928).
Quantum field theory: one of the founders, introduced the concept of second quantization, allowing the description of particle creation and annihilation.
Pascual Jordan.quote: "Quantum mechanics is not just a theory, it is a new way of thinking about reality."
Pascual Jordan.fun_fact: Despite his enormous contribution to quantum mechanics, Jordan remained in the shadow of his colleagues Born and Heisenberg, and his reputation suffered because of his political views. His works were rediscovered only at the end of the 20th century with the development of quantum computing.

Felix Bloch.name: Felix Bloch
Felix Bloch.description: Swiss-American physicist, Nobel laureate. He created the theory of energy bands in solids (Bloch waves), and developed the Bloch equations for nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR). His name is borne by the Bloch sphere — a geometric representation of a qubit state, which has become a fundamental tool in quantum computing and information.
Felix Bloch.biography: He studied physics at the Zurich Polytechnic and at the University of Leipzig under Werner Heisenberg. He worked at Stanford University, where he founded the school of solid-state physics. During the war, he participated in the Manhattan Project. He won the Nobel Prize in Physics (1952) for the development of nuclear magnetic resonance methods. He was the first Director-General of CERN (1954–1955).
key_discoveries: Bloch waves: described the behavior of electrons in a periodic crystal potential, laying the foundation for band theory (1928).
Bloch equations: a phenomenological model of magnetization dynamics in NMR, key to spectroscopy and medical imaging (1946).
Bloch sphere: representation of states of a two-level quantum system (qubit) as points on the unit sphere, fundamental in quantum information.
Felix Bloch.quote: "There is no such thing as a free lunch — this applies to information in the quantum world as well."
Felix Bloch.fun_fact: The Bloch sphere was first introduced in 1946 to describe spin dynamics, but became an icon of quantum computing only half a century later. Bloch also enjoyed music and played the piano.

Isidor Isaac Rabi.name: Isidor Isaac Rabi
Isidor Isaac Rabi.description: American physicist, Nobel laureate. He developed the magnetic resonance method for studying atomic nuclei, and discovered Rabi oscillations — coherent transitions between quantum states under the action of a resonant field. Rabi oscillations are the basic mechanism for controlling qubits in modern quantum processors.
Isidor Isaac Rabi.biography: Born in Austria-Hungary, he emigrated to the USA. He studied at Cornell and Columbia Universities. He worked at Columbia University, where he built a strong school of atomic physics. During the war, he participated in radar development and the Manhattan Project. He won the Nobel Prize in Physics (1944) for his resonance method for measuring nuclear magnetic moments. He served as a science advisor to the US President.
key_discoveries: Magnetic resonance method: developed a technique for precise measurement of nuclear magnetic moments (1937).
Rabi oscillations: theoretically described and experimentally observed periodic quantum transitions in two-level systems, underlying the operation of qubits.
Atomic clocks: his methods led to the creation of the first atomic frequency standards.
Isidor Isaac Rabi.quote: "Physics has filled the world not only with new devices, but also with new ideas."
Isidor Isaac Rabi.fun_fact: Rabi was one of the few scientists who insisted on the peaceful use of atomic energy and opposed the arms race. His student Julian Schwinger became one of the creators of quantum electrodynamics.

Alexander Holevo.name: Alexander Holevo
Alexander Holevo.description: Russian mathematician and physicist, one of the pioneers of quantum information theory. He proved the theorem on the capacity of a quantum channel (the Holevo bound), which determines the maximum amount of classical information that can be extracted from a quantum state. His works laid the foundations of quantum communication and cryptography.
Alexander Holevo.biography: He graduated from the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology (MIPT). He works at the Steklov Mathematical Institute of the Russian Academy of Sciences. He is a winner of international awards, including the Shannon Award (2016) and the Dirac Medal (2025, jointly). He is a corresponding member of the Russian Academy of Sciences. He is the author of the fundamental monograph 'Introduction to Quantum Information Theory'.
key_discoveries: Holevo bound: established an upper limit on the amount of accessible classical information from quantum measurements (1973).
Quantum estimation theory: developed methods for optimal processing of quantum information.
Quantum channel capacity: made decisive contributions to the analysis of the capabilities of quantum communication channels.
Alexander Holevo.quote: "Quantum information is not just information encoded in quantum states; it is a new resource that surpasses the classical one."
Alexander Holevo.fun_fact: Holevo is one of the most cited Russian scientists in quantum information. His bound is often compared to Shannon's capacity in classical information theory.

Peter Zoller.name: Peter Zoller
Peter Zoller.description: Austrian theoretical physicist, one of the fathers of quantum computing. Together with Ignacio Cirac, he proposed the first realistic scheme for a two-qubit gate on trapped ions (Cirac–Zoller gate). His works cover quantum optics, quantum simulation, and quantum networks, becoming a cornerstone of modern quantum technologies.
Peter Zoller.biography: He studied physics at the University of Innsbruck, where he currently works. He had internships at Harvard and MIT. He is the founder of the Institute for Quantum Optics and Quantum Information in Innsbruck. He is a winner of the Wolf Prize in Physics (2013), the Planck Medal, and other awards. He is a member of many academies.
key_discoveries: Cirac–Zoller gate: proposed a mechanism for entangling ion qubits using laser pulses, key to quantum processors (1995).
Quantum simulation: developed concepts for simulating many-body quantum systems using ultracold atoms and ions.
Quantum networks: contributed to the theory of quantum repeaters and distributed quantum computing.
Peter Zoller.quote: "A quantum computer is not just a faster calculator; it is a window into the world of quantum correlations."
Peter Zoller.fun_fact: Zoller is an avid mountaineer and often compares conquering mountain peaks to solving complex physics problems. His work on ion traps is used in commercial quantum computers.

Seth Lloyd.name: Seth Lloyd
Seth Lloyd.description: American physicist and engineer, pioneer of quantum computing and quantum machine learning. Proposed the first constructive models of a quantum computer, introduced the concept of quantum machine learning, and showed how quantum systems can efficiently solve data analysis problems. Author of popular books on quantum information.
Seth Lloyd.biography: Studied at Harvard and Cambridge, received PhD from Rockefeller University. Professor at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). Worked at Bell Labs and IBM. Winner of an innovation award and author of the bestseller 'Programming the Universe'.
key_discoveries: Quantum computer: in 1993 described a realistic molecular quantum computer, stimulating experimental searches.
Quantum machine learning: developed algorithms that use quantum resources to speed up learning, for example, clustering and classification.
Quantum thermodynamics: studied the fundamental limits of computation from the perspective of entropy and energy.
Seth Lloyd.quote: The universe is a quantum computer that computes itself.
Seth Lloyd.fun_fact: Lloyd consults technology companies and government agencies on quantum technologies. He also plays the saxophone and claims that jazz improvisations resemble quantum randomness.

Scott Aaronson.name: Scott Aaronson
Scott Aaronson.description: American computer scientist, leading theorist of quantum complexity and quantum computing. Proved a number of fundamental theorems about the power of quantum computers, including formal arguments for quantum supremacy. Founder of the influential blog 'Shtetl-Optimized', science communicator.
Scott Aaronson.biography: Graduated from Cornell University, received PhD from the University of California, Berkeley. Professor at the University of Texas at Austin. Worked at MIT and the Institute for Advanced Study. Winner of the Alan T. Waterman Award (2012) and the Tomassoni Prize for science communication.
key_discoveries: Quantum supremacy: formally showed that certain computational problems can be solved exponentially faster by a quantum computer, which underlies modern experiments.
Complexity theory: established connections between quantum computing and classical complexity, proving that some problems remain hard even for quantum machines.
Quantum skepticism: critically analyzes claims about quantum computing, separating real achievements from hype.
Scott Aaronson.quote: If your theory of fundamental physics doesn't contain information, you're probably missing something important.
Scott Aaronson.fun_fact: Aaronson is known for his witty and sometimes provocative essays; he proposed the 'Aaronson's kettle' thought experiment against the many-worlds interpretation. His blog is read by tens of thousands of scientists.

Arnold Sommerfeld.name: Arnold Sommerfeld
Arnold Sommerfeld.description: German theoretical physicist, teacher and mentor of a whole generation of creators of quantum mechanics. Introduced fine structure and the principal quantum number into the Bohr atom model, developed the theory of metals and relativistic quantum effects. The fine-structure constant—a dimensionless constant that determines the strength of electromagnetic interaction—is named after him.
Arnold Sommerfeld.biography: Studied at the University of Königsberg, worked at the universities of Göttingen, Munich, and others. His students included Heisenberg, Pauli, Debye, Bethe. Never received the Nobel Prize, although he was nominated more than 80 times. Author of the epochal course 'Lectures on Theoretical Physics'.
key_discoveries: Bohr–Sommerfeld atom model: extended Bohr's theory by introducing elliptical orbits and relativistic corrections, which explained the fine structure of spectra (1916).
Fine-structure constant: first interpreted α ≈ 1/137 as a fundamental constant characterizing electromagnetic interaction.
Theory of metals: created the quantum theory of free electrons in metals, explaining their conductivity.
Arnold Sommerfeld.quote: What is quantum theory? It is philosophy expressed in the language of mathematics.
Arnold Sommerfeld.fun_fact: Sommerfeld is often called the 'eternal runner-up'—he mentored a record number of Nobel laureates but did not receive the prize himself. He was also a talented pianist and music lover.

Abner Shimony.name: Abner Shimony
Abner Shimony.description: American physicist and philosopher of science, one of the pioneers of quantum foundations and quantum information. Formulated the concept of 'entanglement as a resource' and proposed the idea of teleportation of quantum states as early as 1984. Contributed to the interpretation of quantum mechanics and the philosophy of science, bridging physics and metaphysics.
Abner Shimony.biography: Studied at Yale University, received a dual PhD in physics and philosophy from Princeton. Worked at Boston University, collaborated with John Bell and others. Served as president of the International Society for Quantum Foundations.
key_discoveries: Quantum teleportation: together with colleagues proposed the principle of teleporting an unknown quantum state using entanglement (published later, but ideas were discussed earlier).
Shimony paradox: contributed to the analysis of thought experiments demonstrating quantum nonlocality.
Philosophy of quantum mechanics: developed the concept of 'experimental metaphysics', according to which physics can clarify philosophical questions.
Abner Shimony.quote: Quantum mechanics tells us not only what we can know, but also what can be.
Abner Shimony.fun_fact: Shimony was a rabbi and believed that quantum paradoxes have deep parallels with Kabbalistic ideas about the nature of reality. He translated the works of Bohr and Heisenberg into English.

William Unruh.name: William Unruh
William Unruh.description: Canadian physicist, known for the discovery of the Unruh effect: an accelerated observer in a vacuum perceives thermal radiation with a temperature proportional to the acceleration. This phenomenon links quantum field theory with general relativity and has profound implications for black holes and quantum gravity.
William Unruh.biography: Born in the USA, but spent most of his career in Canada. Studied at Princeton, received PhD under the supervision of John Wheeler. Professor at the University of British Columbia. Winner of numerous awards, fellow of the Royal Society of Canada and the Royal Society of London.
key_discoveries: Unruh effect: predicted that a uniformly accelerated detector in a vacuum registers particles with a thermal spectrum (1976).
Analog gravity: pioneer in using hydrodynamic and condensed matter systems to simulate the curvature of spacetime.
Black holes and information: contributed to the understanding of the information loss paradox and proposed a model of 'quiet' black holes.
William Unruh.quote: What is emptiness for one observer is a thermal bath of particles for another. Reality depends on motion.
William Unruh.fun_fact: Unruh is an avid cyclist and uses a bicycle racing analogy to explain the effect: 'If you ride fast enough, you get hot from quantum fluctuations.' The Unruh effect has not yet been experimentally confirmed due to extremely small temperatures.

Leonhard Euler.name: Leonhard Euler
Leonhard Euler.description: Leonard Euler made fundamental contributions to mathematics and physics. His formula e^(ix) = cos x + i sin x, linking trigonometric functions with the exponential function, became a cornerstone of complex analysis. It allowed the unification of approaches to solving differential equations, describing wave processes and electrical circuits. Euler also developed the foundations of the calculus of variations, laid the foundations of graph theory (the Königsberg bridge problem) and introduced many modern mathematical notations.
Leonhard Euler.biography: Studied at the University of Basel under Johann Bernoulli. Worked at the St. Petersburg Academy of Sciences (1727–1741 and from 1766) and the Berlin Academy (1741–1766). Collaborated with Christian Goldbach and other scientists. Was an honorary member of many academies, including the Royal Society of London. Awarded the Order of St. Stanislaus and a prize of the Paris Academy of Sciences.
key_discoveries: Euler's formula: e^(ix) = cos x + i sin x, connecting complex exponentials with trigonometric functions, fundamental for complex analysis and physics.
Solution of the Basel problem: finding the sum of the reciprocals of squares (∑ 1/n² = π²/6), which was a triumph of analysis.
Euler characteristic of polyhedra: formula V − E + F = 2, which laid the foundations of topology.
Introduction of notations f(x), e, i, π and others, widely used to this day.
Leonhard Euler.quote: The art of asking questions is more important than the ability to solve them.
Leonhard Euler.fun_fact: Euler continued to work actively even after total loss of vision, dictating to assistants more than 400 articles and books, which constitutes almost half of his scientific legacy.

George Gabriel Stokes.name: George Gabriel Stokes
George Gabriel Stokes.description: George Gabriel Stokes made significant contributions to mathematical physics and fluid mechanics. His name is associated with Stokes' theorem, which unifies several integral theorems and is widely applied in electrodynamics and hydrodynamics. He also formulated the Navier-Stokes equations, describing the motion of viscous fluids, and discovered the phenomenon of fluorescence (Stokes shift).
George Gabriel Stokes.biography: Graduated from Cambridge University (Pembroke College). From 1849 to 1903 he held the post of Lucasian Professor of Mathematics at Cambridge. Collaborated with William Thomson (Lord Kelvin) in the fields of hydrodynamics and optics. Was president of the Royal Society (1885–1890), awarded the Copley Medal (1893).
key_discoveries: Stokes' theorem: generalizes Green's and Ostrogradsky-Gauss theorems, linking the integral of the curl of a vector field over a surface to the contour integral over its boundary.
Navier-Stokes equations: fundamental equations of motion of a viscous fluid, underlying modern hydrodynamics.
Stokes' law for the drag force on a sphere in a viscous fluid: F = 6πμrv.
Stokes shift in fluorescence: explained that the wavelength of emitted light is longer than the wavelength of absorbed light.
George Gabriel Stokes.quote: The more we study the works of the Creator, the more we see the wisdom and goodness with which they were created.
George Gabriel Stokes.fun_fact: Stokes was not only an outstanding scientist but also actively participated in politics: he was elected as a Member of Parliament for Cambridge University from 1887 to 1892.

Carl Friedrich Gauss.name: Carl Friedrich Gauss
Carl Friedrich Gauss.description: Carl Friedrich Gauss is one of the greatest mathematicians of all time. He made decisive contributions to number theory, algebra, statistics, geometry and astronomy. Gauss's theorem (the divergence theorem) relates the flux of a vector field through a closed surface to the volume integral of the divergence and is key in electromagnetism and gravitation. His work on the curvature of surfaces anticipated non-Euclidean geometry.
Carl Friedrich Gauss.biography: Born in Brunswick, studied at the University of Göttingen. Received his doctorate from the University of Helmstedt. Worked as director of the Göttingen Observatory and professor of astronomy. Collaborated with Wilhelm Weber, with whom he built the first electromagnetic telegraph. Member of many academies, winner of the Copley Medal.
key_discoveries: Fundamental theorem of algebra: proof that any polynomial has a complex root.
Method of least squares and the normal distribution (Gaussian distribution), the foundation of modern statistics.
Gauss's theorem (divergence theorem): ∮ₛ F·dS = ∭ᵥ div F dV, fundamental for vector analysis.
Investigations on the curvature of surfaces (Theorema Egregium), which laid the foundations of differential geometry and non-Euclidean geometry.
Prediction of the orbit of Ceres using computational methods he developed.
Carl Friedrich Gauss.quote: Mathematics is the queen of the sciences, and number theory is the queen of mathematics.
Carl Friedrich Gauss.fun_fact: Gauss himself requested that a regular 17-gon be engraved on his tombstone, the construction of which he found at the age of 19, but the stonemason refused, saying it would be indistinguishable from a circle.

Pythagoras.name: Pythagoras
Pythagoras.description: Pythagoras was an ancient Greek philosopher and mathematician, founder of the Pythagorean school. He is credited with the first proof of the theorem that bears his name, although the relation was known earlier in Babylon. His teaching combined mathematics, music and mysticism; the Pythagoreans believed that numbers are the basis of all things and discovered the connection between harmonic intervals and simple numerical ratios.
Pythagoras.biography: Born on the island of Samos, travelled extensively through Egypt and Babylon, studying secret knowledge. Around 530 BC he founded a community in Croton (southern Italy), where he and his disciples conducted scientific and philosophical research. The school was closed after a revolt, and Pythagoras himself likely died in Metapontum.
key_discoveries: Pythagorean theorem: a² + b² = c² for a right-angled triangle – one of the most famous mathematical statements.
Doctrine of numerical ratios in music: the intervals of the octave, perfect fifth and perfect fourth correspond to ratios 2:1, 3:2 and 4:3.
Discovery of incommensurability (irrational numbers) exemplified by the diagonal of a square.
The concept of 'harmony of the spheres' – the idea that celestial bodies move according to mathematical laws and produce an unheard music.
Pythagoras.fun_fact: Pythagoras imposed strict rules on his followers, including a ban on eating beans; according to one legend, he died because he refused to flee through a bean field.

Emile Clapeyron.name: Emile Clapeyron
Emile Clapeyron.description: Émile Clapeyron was a French physicist and engineer, one of the founders of thermodynamics. He was the first to formulate the ideal gas equation of state in the form PV = nRT, combining the laws of Boyle-Mariotte, Charles, and Gay-Lussac. In addition, he derived the Clapeyron equation for phase transitions, which was later generalized by Clausius, and helped disseminate Carnot's ideas about heat engines.
Emile Clapeyron.biography: Graduated from the École Polytechnique in Paris, then the École des Mines. Worked in Russia (1820–1830), teaching at the St. Petersburg Institute of Railway Engineers; participated in the design of bridges and tunnels. Returning to France, he worked on steam engines and thermodynamics. Member of the Paris Academy of Sciences (1848).
key_discoveries: Ideal gas equation of state: PV = nRT, a synthesis of empirical gas laws into a single formula.
Clapeyron equation for phase transitions: relates the change in pressure and temperature at phase equilibrium, which formed the basis for the Clausius-Clapeyron equation.
Analysis of the operation of heat engines in a memoir of 1834, where he reformulated Carnot's ideas in more rigorous mathematical language.
Engineering developments: calculation and construction of suspension bridges, introduction of steam traction on railways.
Emile Clapeyron.fun_fact: Clapeyron's fate was tragic: his wife died young, and he raised four daughters alone, continuing scientific work with almost no recognition during his lifetime.

Ralph Fowler.name: Ralph Fowler
Ralph Fowler.description: One of the founders of statistical thermodynamics and theoretical astrophysics. Formulated the zeroth law of thermodynamics, contributed to the theory of white dwarfs and quantum statistics.
Ralph Fowler.biography: Born in Roydon, Essex. Studied at the University of Cambridge (Trinity College). During the First World War served in the naval artillery. Worked in Cambridge, where he collaborated with P. A. M. Dirac, E. A. Milne, S. Chandrasekhar. Was the scientific advisor of Chandrasekhar. Married the daughter of Ernest Rutherford. Awards: Hughes Medal (1924), Royal Medal (1936).
key_discoveries: Formulated the zeroth law of thermodynamics as a postulate of the transitivity of thermal equilibrium.
Together with L. W. Nordheim explained field electron emission (tunneling) via the Fowler-Nordheim model.
Applied statistical mechanics to degenerate electron gas in white dwarfs, laying the foundation for the theory of compact stars.
Developed the theory of chain reactions in stellar interiors.
Ralph Fowler.fun_fact: Was the son-in-law of Ernest Rutherford and father-in-law of G. I. Taylor.

Hendrik Casimir.name: Hendrik Casimir
Hendrik Casimir.description: Dutch theoretical physicist, known for predicting the Casimir effect — an attractive force between uncharged conducting plates in a vacuum, arising from quantum fluctuations. Also contributed to low-temperature physics and superconductivity.
Hendrik Casimir.biography: Born in The Hague. Studied at Leiden University, where he worked with Paul Ehrenfest. Then conducted research at the Philips laboratory in Eindhoven, where he worked most of his career. Collaborated with Niels Bohr, discussing the nature of van der Waals forces. Served as president of the European Physical Society. Awarded the Matteucci Medal and other honors.
key_discoveries: Predicted the Casimir effect (1948) — attraction between conducting plates in vacuum due to the difference in zero-point energy of the electromagnetic field.
Together with Dirk Polder described the Casimir–Polder force between an atom and a conducting surface.
Developed the foundations of the thermodynamics of superconductors and Casimir physics.
Hendrik Casimir.quote: The Casimir effect is a manifestation of the reality of zero-point fluctuations of the vacuum.
Hendrik Casimir.fun_fact: Wrote poetry and was part of an informal poetry society 'The Inkwell Club'.

James Jeans.name: James Jeans
James Jeans.description: British physicist, astronomer, and mathematician. Best known for his work on gravitational instability (Jeans instability), which explains the formation of stars from interstellar clouds. Also, in collaboration with Rayleigh, derived the Rayleigh–Jeans law for thermal radiation.
James Jeans.biography: Born in Ormskirk, Lancashire. Studied at Trinity College, Cambridge. Taught at Cambridge and Princeton, then worked at the Royal Institution. Author of popular books 'The Universe Around Us' and 'The Mysterious Universe'. Awarded the Royal Medal and other distinctions.
key_discoveries: Formulated the criterion of gravitational instability (Jeans instability), determining the minimum mass of a cloud capable of collapsing into a star.
Together with Lord Rayleigh derived the blackbody radiation law for the long-wavelength region (Rayleigh–Jeans law).
Developed the theory of equilibrium figures of rotating fluid bodies in astrophysics.
James Jeans.quote: The universe begins to look more like a great thought than a great machine.
James Jeans.fun_fact: Was a talented organist and often gave concerts.

Josef Lense.name: Josef Lense
Josef Lense.description: Austrian physicist and mathematician, co-author of the Lense–Thirring effect — the prediction of the dragging of inertial reference frames by a rotating massive body in general relativity.
Josef Lense.biography: Born in Vienna. Studied at the University of Vienna, where he later became a professor of mathematics. In 1918, together with Hans Thirring, published a paper on the influence of rotating massive bodies on spacetime metric. Worked in the field of differential geometry and mathematical physics.
key_discoveries: Together with Hans Thirring calculated the effect of dragging inertial frames (Lense–Thirring effect) within the framework of general relativity.
Josef Lense.fun_fact: The effect was long considered too small to measure, but was confirmed by the Gravity Probe B satellite in 2004.

Rudolf Mossbauer.name: Rudolf Mossbauer
Rudolf Mossbauer.description: German physicist who discovered the Mössbauer effect — the recoil-free emission and absorption of gamma rays in a crystal lattice. Nobel Prize laureate in Physics (1961). The effect enabled the creation of ultra-high resolution spectroscopy.
Rudolf Mossbauer.biography: Born in Munich. Studied at the Technical University of Munich. In 1958, while a graduate student, made his discovery. Worked at Caltech and Munich. Awarded the Nobel Prize at the age of 32.
key_discoveries: Discovered recoilless resonant absorption of gamma radiation by nuclei (Mössbauer effect), which became the basis of Mössbauer spectroscopy.
Developed precision methods for measuring hyperfine interactions.
Rudolf Mossbauer.fun_fact: The discovery was so unexpected that the first paper about it was rejected by a scientific journal.

John Henry Poynting.name: John Henry Poynting
John Henry Poynting.description: British physicist, best known for his work on Poynting's theorem and the Poynting vector, which describe the flow of energy in an electromagnetic field. His research laid the foundation for understanding electromagnetic energy transfer and influenced the development of electrical engineering and physics. He also contributed to the study of light pressure and predicted the Poynting–Robertson effect, which explains the motion of dust particles around stars.
John Henry Poynting.biography: John Henry Poynting was born on 9 September 1852 in Monton, England. He studied at Owens College, Manchester, and Trinity College, Cambridge, where he became a fellow. From 1880, he worked as a professor of physics at Mason Science College (later the University of Birmingham), where he established a strong physics laboratory. He collaborated with J. J. Thomson and other renowned scientists. He was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society of London in 1888 and awarded the Royal Medal in 1905. He died on 30 March 1914.
key_discoveries: Poynting's theorem and the Poynting vector (description of the energy flow of the electromagnetic field, a fundamental concept in electrodynamics)
Poynting–Robertson effect (the drag on microscopic particles in the Solar System caused by solar light pressure)
Measurements of the gravitational constant and investigations into the elasticity of materials
Work on light pressure and the transfer of momentum by radiation
John Henry Poynting.fun_fact: Poynting was keen on astronomy and even built his own observatory in his garden; his interest in celestial mechanics led to the prediction of the cosmic dust effect.

Klaus von Klitzing.name: Klaus von Klitzing
Klaus von Klitzing.description: German physicist, Nobel laureate for the discovery of the integer quantum Hall effect. This phenomenon demonstrated the quantization of electrical resistance in two-dimensional electron systems at low temperatures and strong magnetic fields, which became fundamental for metrology and the understanding of quantum states of matter. His work underlies modern quantum resistance standards.
Klaus von Klitzing.biography: Klaus von Klitzing was born on 28 June 1943 in Schrotta, Germany (now Poland). He studied physics at the Technical University of Braunschweig and received his doctorate from the University of Würzburg. He worked at the Physikalisch-Technische Bundesanstalt (PTB) and at the Max Planck Institute for Solid State Research, where he later became director. In 1980, while conducting experiments with silicon MOSFETs, he discovered the quantization of the Hall resistance. For this discovery he was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1985. He continues to be active in research and teaching.
key_discoveries: Integer quantum Hall effect (quantization of the Hall resistance with high precision)
Creation of a precision electrical resistance standard based on the quantum effect
Investigations of two-dimensional electron systems and strong magnetic fields
Klaus von Klitzing.fun_fact: The discovery of the quantum Hall effect happened by chance during a lunch break: von Klitzing decided to measure the resistance of a sample at a very low temperature and was amazed by the step-like behavior of the graph.

Rainer Sachs.name: Rainer Sachs
Rainer Sachs.description: American astrophysicist of German origin, one of the authors of the Sachs–Wolfe effect, which explains large-scale temperature fluctuations in the cosmic microwave background by gravitational perturbations in the expanding Universe. He also contributed to the theory of gravitational lensing, deriving equations that describe the distortion of light in the Universe. His work has become key in modern cosmology.
Rainer Sachs.biography: Rainer K. Sachs was born on 13 June 1932 in Frankfurt am Main, Germany. He emigrated to the USA, where he earned his doctorate at Syracuse University. He worked at the University of California, Berkeley, and at other research institutions. Together with Arthur M. Wolfe in 1967, he predicted the effect linking the anisotropy of the cosmic microwave background to gravitational potentials. His research covered general relativity, cosmology, and mathematical physics. He passed away on 16 January 2024.
key_discoveries: Sachs–Wolfe effect (the influence of gravitational perturbations on the temperature of the cosmic microwave background)
Sachs equations for light propagation in curved spacetime (gravitational lensing)
Contribution to the theory of structure formation in the Universe
Rainer Sachs.fun_fact: Sachs began his scientific career in theoretical biology before switching to cosmology, where he achieved outstanding results.

Leonard Mandel.name: Leonard Mandel
Leonard Mandel.description: British-American physicist, one of the pioneers of quantum optics. He is best known as a co-discoverer of the Hong–Ou–Mandel effect, which demonstrates two-photon interference and the indistinguishability of quantum particles. His experiments and theoretical work laid the foundation for quantum information and quantum computing. He made significant contributions to statistical optics and the study of non-classical light.
Leonard Mandel.biography: Leonard Mandel was born on 9 May 1927 in Berlin, Germany. Due to his Jewish background, his family emigrated to England and then to the USA. He earned his doctorate at the University of London. He worked at Imperial College London and then at the University of Rochester, where he spent most of his career. Together with Chun Ki Hong and Zheyu Ou in 1987, he experimentally observed the effect of two-photon interference. He was awarded the Max Born Award and other honors. He died on 9 February 2001.
key_discoveries: Hong–Ou–Mandel effect (two-photon interference demonstrating quantum entanglement and photon indistinguishability)
Experimental investigations of coherence and photon statistics
Work on non-classical light and squeezed states, including the first experiment to produce squeezed light
Leonard Mandel.fun_fact: Mandel became interested in optics after attending a lecture by Dennis Gabor, the inventor of holography, which determined his future scientific path.

Charles-Augustin de Coulomb.name: Charles-Augustin de Coulomb
Charles-Augustin de Coulomb.description: French military engineer and physicist, best known for discovering Coulomb's law, which describes the force of electrostatic interaction between charged particles. His work laid the foundations of electrostatics and the theory of elasticity.
Charles-Augustin de Coulomb.biography: Charles-Augustin de Coulomb was born in Angoulême, France. He studied at the Paris School of Engineering in Mézières, after which he served as a military engineer in the West Indies. Returning to France, he held various engineering positions and worked on fortification construction. In 1781 he became a member of the French Academy of Sciences. During the Revolution, he retired to Blois, where he continued his scientific research. His main work—memoirs on electricity and magnetism—was published between 1785 and 1789. He also studied friction, the stiffness of threads, and the operation of windmills.
key_discoveries: Coulomb's law: the force of electrostatic interaction between two point charges is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
Experiments with the torsion balance to measure small forces, enabling quantitative investigation of electrical and magnetic interactions.
Research on friction and adhesion in mechanics, establishing laws of dry friction.
Work on the theory of elasticity, calculation of torsion of wires and threads.
Charles-Augustin de Coulomb.fun_fact: Coulomb built his famous torsion balance from improvised materials, including a silk thread, and used it to measure extremely small forces, which allowed him to discover the law of interaction of charges.

Georg Ohm.name: Georg Ohm
Georg Ohm.description: German physicist who discovered the fundamental law of electric circuits—Ohm's law, relating voltage, current, and resistance. His work laid the foundation for quantitative analysis of electric circuits.
Georg Ohm.biography: Georg Simon Ohm was born in Erlangen, the son of a locksmith. He received home education, then studied at the University of Erlangen, but did not complete the course due to financial difficulties. He worked as a mathematics and physics teacher in various schools. In 1826 he published his main work, “Determination of the Law According to which Metals Conduct Contact Electricity,” in which he formulated Ohm's law. His discovery was not immediately recognized; it was only in 1841 that the Royal Society of London awarded him the Copley Medal. From 1849 he was a professor at the University of Munich.
key_discoveries: Ohm's law: the current in a conductor is directly proportional to the applied voltage and inversely proportional to the resistance (I = V/R).
Introduction of the concepts of electromotive force, voltage drop, and resistance as characteristics of a conductor.
Experimental investigation of the conductivity of various metals and the effect of temperature on resistance.
Georg Ohm.fun_fact: Initially, the scientific community rejected Ohm's law, and he had to resign from his school position. Recognition came only years later, after experiments by other scientists confirmed his conclusions.

Willebrord Snellius.name: Willebrord Snellius
Willebrord Snellius.description: Dutch mathematician, astronomer, and physicist who discovered the law of refraction of light, known as Snell's law. His work in geodesy and navigation was also significant.
Willebrord Snellius.biography: Willebrord Snellius (Latinized name Snellius) was born in Leiden, the son of a mathematician. He studied law and mathematics at Leiden University, then traveled through Europe, communicating with scientists. Upon returning, he became professor of mathematics at Leiden University. He worked on geodesy, introducing the method of triangulation for measuring the arc of the meridian. He experimentally discovered the law of refraction in 1621, but did not publish it; the law was publicized by Descartes in 1637. He died at the age of 46.
key_discoveries: Snell's law: the ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is constant for a given pair of media and equals the relative refractive index (n1 sin θ1 = n2 sin θ2).
Development of the triangulation method for precise distance measurement on the ground, which allowed refining the length of a degree of the meridian.
Astronomical and navigational calculations, improvement of tables for marine navigation.
Willebrord Snellius.fun_fact: Snellius discovered the law of refraction while observing the apparent bending of a stick placed in water, but did not publish it; it was only in 1703 that manuscripts confirmed his priority.

Daniel Bernoulli.name: Daniel Bernoulli
Daniel Bernoulli.description: Swiss mathematician and physicist, one of the founders of hydrodynamics. Bernoulli's principle describes the relationship between fluid flow speed and pressure, explaining wing lift and many natural phenomena.
Daniel Bernoulli.biography: Daniel Bernoulli was born in Groningen, the son of the eminent mathematician Johann Bernoulli. He studied at the University of Basel, where he studied medicine, but under his father's influence became interested in mathematics. In 1725 he accepted an invitation to the St. Petersburg Academy of Sciences, where he worked with Leonhard Euler. In 1733 he returned to Basel, where he became a professor of anatomy, botany, and later physics and philosophy. His main work, “Hydrodynamics” (1738), laid the foundations of the kinetic theory of gases and hydrodynamics.
key_discoveries: Bernoulli's principle: in a steady flow of a liquid or gas, the sum of static and dynamic pressure remains constant; an increase in flow speed leads to a decrease in pressure.
Kinetic theory of gases: a model of a gas as a collection of randomly moving particles, explaining pressure through molecular impacts on the walls.
Work on the theory of vibrating strings, probability theory, and mathematical analysis (Bernoulli's equation).
Daniel Bernoulli.fun_fact: Daniel Bernoulli had to leave St. Petersburg due to the harsh climate, but his work in Russia led to the creation of hydrodynamics.

Archimedes.name: Archimedes
Archimedes.description: Ancient Greek mathematician, physicist, and engineer, one of the greatest scientists of antiquity. Archimedes' principle explains the buoyancy of bodies and is widely applied in hydrostatics.
Archimedes.biography: Archimedes was born in Syracuse on the island of Sicily, presumably into an astronomer's family. He studied in Alexandria, where he became acquainted with the works of Euclid. Returning to Syracuse, he devoted himself to science, creating mechanisms and mathematical theories. During the siege of Syracuse by the Romans, he led the defense, using war machines. He was killed by a Roman soldier. His works have come down to us in the form of treatises: “On the Sphere and Cylinder,” “On Floating Bodies,” and others.
key_discoveries: Archimedes' principle: a body immersed in a fluid or gas experiences a buoyant force equal to the weight of the displaced medium.
The method of exhaustion for calculating areas and volumes of curved figures, anticipating integral calculus.
The lever principle, systems of pulleys, and the endless screw (Archimedes' screw) for raising water.
Accurate approximation of the number π and quadrature of the parabola.
Archimedes.quote: Give me a place to stand, and I will move the Earth.
Archimedes.fun_fact: According to legend, Archimedes discovered his famous principle while taking a bath and ran out into the street shouting “Eureka!”, forgetting to dress.

Augustin-Louis Cauchy.description: Augustin-Louis Cauchy was a French mathematician who transformed analysis from an intuitive art into a rigorous science. He gave precise definitions of limit, continuity, and derivative, built the foundations of complex analysis, and left a mark on mechanics and elasticity theory. In terms of the number of theorems named after him, he has no equal.
Augustin-Louis Cauchy.biography: Born in Paris at the height of the Revolution, studied at the École Polytechnique and the School of Bridges and Roads. He was a professor at the École Polytechnique, the Sorbonne, and the Collège de France. After the July Revolution, he voluntarily went into exile, worked in Turin and Prague, but later returned. Knight of the Legion of Honour.
key_discoveries: Rigorous definition of limit, continuity, and derivative: replaced intuitive notions with formal definitions in ε-δ language, laying the foundation for modern mathematical analysis.
Cauchy's integral theorem and residue theory: the foundation of complex analysis, enabling the computation of complex integrals and having applications in quantum field theory and hydrodynamics.
Cauchy stress tensor: introduced the concept of a tensor in continuum mechanics, describing the deformation forces inside a body.
Augustin-Louis Cauchy.quote: A man should not be proud of what he knows, but proud of what he does.
Augustin-Louis Cauchy.fun_fact: Cauchy's incredible prolificness is legendary: he wrote about 800 articles, and the Paris Academy had to limit the volume of publications because the budget could not withstand the printing of his endless works. It is said that his last words were: "Man passes away, but the formulas remain."

Bernhard Riemann.description: Bernhard Riemann was a mathematical genius whose ideas transformed geometry and analysis. His concept of curved space became the mathematical foundation for Einstein's general theory of relativity, and the hypothesis about the zeta function remains the greatest unsolved mystery of mathematics.
Bernhard Riemann.biography: Born in the village of Breselenz (Hanover), studied theology, but soon switched to mathematics under the influence of Gauss in Göttingen. Received his doctorate under Gauss's supervision. Was a professor at the University of Göttingen. Died at age 39 of tuberculosis in Italy, where he had gone for treatment.
key_discoveries: Riemannian geometry: a generalization of Euclidean geometry to curved multidimensional spaces using the metric tensor; without it, modern cosmology and the theory of gravity are inconceivable.
Riemann integral: the first rigorous definition of the definite integral, based on the limit of integral sums; it still forms the basis of calculus courses.

Zeta function and Riemann hypothesis: formulated the famous hypothesis about the distribution of zeros of the zeta function, the proof of which became one of the 'Millennium Problems' and remains open.
Bernhard Riemann.quote: Mathematics is not just a tool, but an art that reveals the structure of reality.
Bernhard Riemann.fun_fact: Riemann's historic 1854 lecture "On the Hypotheses That Lie at the Foundation of Geometry" was so groundbreaking that only a few listeners attended, among whom was a deeply impressed Gauss. It took more than half a century for Riemann's ideas to be understood and applied by Einstein.

David Hilbert.description: David Hilbert is one of the greatest mathematicians in history, whose ideas defined the face of modern mathematics and laid the foundation for the mathematical apparatus of quantum mechanics. He developed the theory of Hilbert spaces, formulated the famous 23 problems that became a program for the development of mathematics in the 20th century, and made fundamental contributions to invariant theory, algebraic number theory, and mathematical logic.
David Hilbert.biography: Born in Königsberg (now Kaliningrad), he studied and worked at the University of Königsberg, where he became friends with Hermann Minkowski. From 1895, he was a professor at the University of Göttingen, which under him became a world center of mathematics. Among his students and colleagues were John von Neumann, Hermann Weyl, Emmy Noether. He was a laureate of the Lobachevsky Prize and many other awards. His scientific school became legendary.
key_discoveries: Hilbert space: an abstract vector space with an inner product, which became the mathematical foundation of quantum mechanics (wave function as a state vector).
Hilbert's problems: a list of 23 unsolved problems presented in 1900 that determined the main directions of mathematical research for decades to come (some have been solved, others are still open).
Invariant theory and the basis theorem: proof of the finite generation of invariants, which laid the foundation for modern algebraic geometry.
David Hilbert.quote: We must know — we will know.
David Hilbert.fun_fact: Hilbert lectured slowly and often repeated difficult parts so that even the slowest students could understand the material. On his deathbed, he supposedly said about his 23 problems: 'Put me close to the mathematics department at the cemetery — maybe I'll resurrect.'

Stefan Banach.description: Stefan Banach is a self-taught Polish mathematician, the founder of modern functional analysis and one of the creators of the theory of Banach spaces. His works on measure theory, topology, and operator theory changed the face of mathematics, and his name became a common noun in linear analysis.
Stefan Banach.biography: Born in Kraków, he did not receive formal higher education; he educated himself and attended lectures as an auditor. He was 'discovered' by Hugo Steinhaus, who overheard Banach discussing Lebesgue measure in a Kraków park. Together they created the famous Lwów School of Mathematics. Banach became a professor at the Lwów Polytechnic Institute. He was awarded the Banach Prize (established posthumously) and other honors.
key_discoveries: Banach space: a complete normed vector space — the central concept of functional analysis, without which modern differential equations, quantum mechanics, and numerical methods are impossible.

Banach–Tarski paradox: a theorem about the possibility of decomposing a ball into a finite number of pieces and reassembling them into two balls of the same radius, demonstrating the counterintuitive consequences of the axiom of choice and measure theory.
Fixed-point theorem for contraction mappings: the main tool for proving the existence and uniqueness of solutions in many areas.
Stefan Banach.quote: Mathematics is the most beautiful and most powerful branch of knowledge created by the human spirit.
Stefan Banach.fun_fact: Banach was an avid coffee lover and gourmet. The famous 'Scottish Books' — notebooks where members of the Lwów School of Mathematics wrote down unsolved problems — were often filled out right in the Scottish Café over a cup of coffee and a glass of brandy.

Joseph Fourier.description: Jean-Baptiste Joseph Fourier is a French mathematician and physicist whose name is inextricably linked with harmonic analysis. His theory of Fourier series and transforms became a universal language for describing waves and oscillations, penetrating all corners of physics, engineering, and signal processing.
Joseph Fourier.biography: Born in Auxerre, orphaned early, educated at a military school. He participated in Napoleon's Egyptian campaign, heading the scientific part of the expedition. Upon returning, he became prefect of the Isère department but continued to intensively engage in science. In 1822, he published his magnum opus — 'The Analytical Theory of Heat'. He was elected a member of the French Academy of Sciences and awarded the Legion of Honour.
key_discoveries: Fourier series: representation of any periodic function as a sum of sines and cosines, opening the era of spectral analysis and becoming the basis for solving partial differential equations.
Fourier transform: a mathematical operation that transfers a function from the time domain to the frequency domain — the basis of modern technologies (MP3, JPEG, tomography, radio communication).
Fourier's law of heat conduction: described the spread of heat in solids, linking the heat flux density to the temperature gradient.
Joseph Fourier.quote: Mathematical analysis of nature gives us the ability to understand the secrets of the world.
Joseph Fourier.fun_fact: Fourier was a passionate advocate of heat: he believed that heat is good for health, constantly wrapped himself in clothing, and even in hot Egypt managed to wear a warm overcoat, earning the nickname 'the eccentric from Grenoble'.

Andrey Markov.description: Andrey Andreyevich Markov is a Russian mathematician whose name became a symbol of stochastic dependence. Markov chains — his most famous creation — underlie the theory of random processes and are now used everywhere: from computer algorithms to modeling financial markets.
Andrey Markov.biography: Born in Ryazan, he graduated from St. Petersburg University, where he worked all his life. A student of Pafnuty Chebyshev, Markov became one of the leaders of the St. Petersburg mathematical school. He was an academician of the St. Petersburg Academy of Sciences. He was a principled man: in protest against the excommunication of Leo Tolstoy from the church, he publicly refused all state awards.
key_discoveries: Markov chains: sequences of events in which the future depends only on the present and not on the past; they formalized the concept of a Markov process — the basis of queuing theory, genetics, and machine learning.
Markov's inequality: a simple but powerful tool for estimating the probability of deviation of a random variable, widely used in statistics and measurement theory.
Limit theorems for chains: he justified the convergence of distributions in chains to a stationary regime.
Andrey Markov.quote: Probability is the measure of our ignorance.
Andrey Markov.fun_fact: Markov was an avid walker and once walked from St. Petersburg to Moscow. He first applied his famous chains to a statistical analysis of the alternation of vowels and consonants in the novel 'Eugene Onegin', proving that it is not random.

Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz.description: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz is a universal genius who, simultaneously with Newton (and independently of him), created differential and integral calculus. His ideas permeate mathematics, philosophy, logic, and computer science: the binary number system, the mechanical calculating machine, and the concept of a universal language of science.
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz.biography: Born in Leipzig, he studied philosophy and law at the universities of Leipzig and Jena. He served as a diplomat and advisor at European courts, maintaining extensive correspondence with scientists and monarchs. He founded the Berlin Academy of Sciences and advised Peter the Great on creating an academy in St. Petersburg. He spent the last years of his life in Hanover, working on the history of the House of Welf.
key_discoveries: Differential and integral calculus: the modern system of notation (dx, ∫), rules of differentiation and integration, the fundamental theorem of calculus — the basis of mathematical analysis.
Binary number system: representation of numbers using only zeros and ones, which became the foundation of computer technology.
Principle of pre-established harmony: a philosophical concept explaining the interaction of soul and body, which influenced the development of systems thinking.
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz.quote: We live in the best of all possible worlds.
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz.fun_fact: Leibniz dreamed of creating a characteristica universalis — a universal language that would allow any concept to be expressed strictly, and then resolve any disputes by calculation. Today, his idea is partly embodied by formal logics and programming languages.

Lisa Randall.name: Lisa Randall
Lisa Randall.description: American theoretical physicist, who together with Raman Sundrum created the model of warped extra dimensions (the Randall–Sundrum model), which offers a solution to the hierarchy problem in particle physics. Her work links particle physics, cosmology, and string theory, in particular explaining the weakness of gravity.
Lisa Randall.biography: Lisa Randall was born in 1962 in New York. She received her education at Harvard University, earning her PhD there. She taught at MIT and Princeton, and is currently a professor at Harvard. Author of popular physics books. Member of the National Academy of Sciences. Actively involved in popularizing science.
key_discoveries: Randall–Sundrum model (1999) – suggests the existence of one (RS-1) or more (RS-2) extra spatial dimensions
warped along anti-de Sitter space. The warped geometry allows gravity to vary significantly across the extra dimension
explaining
why it is weaker than the other interactions. The model predicts excitations with masses at the TeV scale
accessible at colliders.
Lisa Randall.quote: Extra dimensions might prove to be not just a mathematical fantasy, but a reality hidden from our eyes.
Lisa Randall.fun_fact: Lisa Randall is one of the most cited theoretical physicists in the world and often appears on lists of influential women in science. She even appeared as herself in the TV series "The Big Bang Theory".

Raman Sundrum.name: Raman Sundrum
Raman Sundrum.description: Indian-American theoretical physicist, known for the warped extra dimensions model jointly developed with Lisa Randall. His work greatly influenced high-energy physics phenomenology, inspiring many experimental searches for signatures of extra dimensions at colliders and in cosmological observations.
Raman Sundrum.biography: Raman Sundrum was born in 1964 in India. He studied in India (IIT Kanpur) and then in the USA (Yale University). He worked at Boston University, Johns Hopkins University, and is now a professor at the University of Maryland. In addition to the Randall–Sundrum model, he contributed to technicolor theory and composite Higgs models.
key_discoveries: Randall–Sundrum model (1999) – joint work

proposing a mechanism of warped spacetime in extra dimensions to solve the hierarchy problem. In the RS-2 variant with even one infinite extra dimension, gravity is effectively localized on the brane. Technicolor theory and ideas about the pseudo-Goldstone nature of the Higgs boson.
Raman Sundrum.quote: Nature loves to hide, but mathematics gives us the keys to her locks.
Raman Sundrum.fun_fact: The name 'Randall–Sundrum' is sometimes shortened to RS model, and physicists joke about 'brane worlds' in the style of science-fiction parallel universes.

R. Brent Tully.name: R. Brent Tully
R. Brent Tully.description: American astronomer, co-author of the Tully–Fisher relation, one of the most important methods for estimating distances to spiral galaxies and studying their dynamics. His work laid the foundation for the modern understanding of dark matter distribution in the local universe and large-scale structure.
R. Brent Tully.biography: Richard Brent Tully was born in 1943. He received his PhD from the University of Maryland. Since 1975, he has worked at the University of Hawaii. He is known not only for the relation but also for the catalog of nearby galaxies, and the discovery of the giant structure – the Pisces–Cetus Supercluster. Member of the National Academy of Sciences.
key_discoveries: Tully–Fisher relation (1977) – empirical relationship between the luminosity of a spiral galaxy and the width of its neutral hydrogen emission line (proportional to rotation speed). Allows distance determination to galaxies
serves as a fundamental test of theories of galaxy formation and evolution
sensitive to dark matter content. Discovery of the Pisces–Cetus Supercluster (1987) – one of the largest known structures in the universe
uniting many galaxy clusters
including the Local Supercluster.
R. Brent Tully.quote: Every galaxy tells a story — you just need to correctly decipher its light.
R. Brent Tully.fun_fact: Tully and Fisher initially discovered their relation by studying galaxy rotation speeds through radio observations. It became a 'ruler' for astronomers and one of the proofs of dark matter existence.

Michel Mayor.name: Michel Mayor
Michel Mayor.description: Swiss astrophysicist, Nobel Prize laureate in Physics (2019) for the discovery of the first exoplanet orbiting a sun-like star – 51 Pegasi b. His work opened a new era in astronomy and led to the development of methods for detecting and studying the atmospheres of distant worlds.
Michel Mayor.biography: Michel Mayor was born in 1942 in Lausanne, Switzerland. He received his PhD from the University of Geneva, where he worked for most of his career. Before discovering exoplanets, he studied binary stars and Galactic dynamics. Together with Didier Queloz, he developed the ELODIE spectrograph, which made it possible to detect the faint wobbles in the star's radial velocity.
key_discoveries: 51 Pegasi b (1995) – first planet outside the Solar System
orbiting a main-sequence star. It is a 'hot Jupiter'
with an orbital period of about 4 days. The radial velocity method
used for the discovery
became standard. Development of high-precision spectrographs (CORALIE
HARPS)
which allowed the discovery of hundreds of other exoplanets
including Earth-type planets.
Michel Mayor.quote: We have discovered a new world, and this is only the beginning of a great journey.
Michel Mayor.fun_fact: When Mayor and Queloz first saw the planet's signal, they thought it was an instrument error and spent months checking before believing the discovery.

Didier Queloz.name: Didier Queloz
Didier Queloz.description: Swiss astronomer, co-discoverer of the first exoplanet orbiting a sun-like star. His instrumental developments and observational campaigns laid the foundation for modern exoplanet science. Nobel Prize laureate in Physics 2019.
Didier Queloz.biography: Didier Queloz was born in 1966 in Switzerland. As a graduate student at the University of Geneva under the supervision of Michel Mayor, he participated in the creation of the ELODIE spectrograph and data analysis. After the discovery, he continued exoplanet research at Cambridge, where he became a professor. He is also a co-founder of several projects searching for extraterrestrial life.
key_discoveries: 51 Pegasi b (1995) – application of high-precision radial velocity measurements
allowing the detection of the star's reflex motion with an amplitude of only 50 m/s. This was a breakthrough
proving
that planets are common in the Galaxy. Co-author and designer of next-generation spectrographs (HARPS
ESPRESSO)
which pushed precision to 10 cm/s
revealing Earth-mass planets.
Didier Queloz.quote: We must never forget that each new planet is an opportunity to understand our place in the Universe.
Didier Queloz.fun_fact: Queloz was still a student when working on the project, and often joked that his main task was 'cleaning contacts on the spectrograph' — so much depended on eliminating noise.

R. A. Rossiter.name: R. A. Rossiter
R. A. Rossiter.description: American astronomer, jointly with D. B. McLaughlin discovered the Rossiter–McLaughlin effect, which allows measuring the projection of a star's rotation onto the line of sight by the distortion of radial velocity curves during eclipses. The effect is widely applied in studies of transiting exoplanets to determine the orientation of planetary orbits relative to the rotation of the parent stars.
R. A. Rossiter.biography: Richard Alfred Rossiter was born in 1886. He was educated at the University of Michigan, where he worked for most of his career at the observatory. He studied binary stars, variable stars, and galactic rotation. His first scientific publication on the effect of radial velocity distortion due to stellar rotation was published in 1924 based on observations of the Beta Lyrae system.
key_discoveries: Rossiter–McLaughlin effect (1924) – change in the profile of spectral lines and radial velocities during the passage of one star across the disk of another
