caused by the rotation of the eclipsed star. Allows determining the angle between the star's rotation axis and the companion's orbital plane
critically important for studying the architecture of exoplanetary systems.
R. A. Rossiter.quote: Observing the rotation of stars through their spectra opened a new era in astrophysics.
R. A. Rossiter.fun_fact: Rossiter published his work on the influence of rotation on radial velocities in the same year as McLaughlin (1924), but they worked independently. The effect was recognized only many years after the first works.

D. B. McLaughlin.name: D. B. McLaughlin
D. B. McLaughlin.description: American astronomer, co-author of the Rossiter–McLaughlin effect. His measurements of radial velocity distortion in the Algol system confirmed that the apparent rotation of a star can be explained by a projective effect during eclipses. He contributed to the spectroscopy of variable stars and the study of line asymmetry.
D. B. McLaughlin.biography: Dean Benjamin McLaughlin was born in 1901. He studied and worked at the University of Michigan, where he became a professor of astronomy. Besides the effect that bears his name, he is known for his research on novae, Galactic structure, and the polarization of light from minor planets. He was an editor of the Astronomical Journal.
key_discoveries: Rossiter–McLaughlin effect – simultaneously with Rossiter, he recorded the asymmetry of radial velocity curves in the eclipsing variable Algol (Beta Persei) and interpreted it as a consequence of stellar rotation. His data became the classic confirmation of the effect.
D. B. McLaughlin.quote: Even stars rotate, and in their light we read their motion.
D. B. McLaughlin.fun_fact: McLaughlin was a versatile scientist – he also studied the polarization properties of planetary and asteroid surfaces, which was a rather rare topic at the time.

Robert H. Dicke.name: Robert H. Dicke
Robert H. Dicke.description: American physicist who made fundamental contributions to gravitational physics, astrophysics, and microwave technology. He developed the Dicke radiometer, which became a key instrument for the discovery of the cosmic microwave background. He independently predicted the existence of relic radiation and conducted experiments to test the equivalence principle.
Robert H. Dicke.biography: Robert Henry Dicke was born in 1916. He graduated from Princeton University and worked there, heading the physics department. During World War II, he worked at the MIT Radiation Laboratory. He contributed to the creation of lasers (co-author of the maser patent). His group at Princeton was searching for the CMB when Penzias and Wilson from Bell Labs accidentally discovered it.
key_discoveries: Dicke radiometer – a highly sensitive microwave receiver using synchronous detection. The basis of radio astronomy observations, used in telescopes to measure the cosmic microwave background (CMB).
Prediction of the relic radiation – several years before the discovery of the CMB, he substantiated the existence of a background from the hot early universe, cooled to the microwave range.
Brans–Dicke theory – a scalar-tensor theory of gravity, an alternative to general relativity, in which the gravitational constant may vary over time.
Robert H. Dicke.quote: The universe was hot, and that must have observable consequences.
Robert H. Dicke.fun_fact: When Penzias and Wilson discovered the CMB, they initially did not know what it was. Dicke, upon learning of their observations, immediately realized that this was the very radiation his group had been preparing to search for.

Roberto D. Peccei.name: Roberto D. Peccei
Roberto D. Peccei.description: Italian theoretical physicist, together with Helen Quinn proposed the mechanism for solving the strong CP problem in quantum chromodynamics, known as the Peccei–Quinn symmetry. This predicted the existence of axions, hypothetical particles that are prime candidates for dark matter.
Roberto D. Peccei.biography: Roberto Peccei was born in Italy in 1942. He graduated from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), then worked at Stanford University, the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA), where he became dean of the physics department and vice-chancellor for research. He made significant contributions to the phenomenology of elementary particle physics. He was a scientific administrator for major projects.
key_discoveries: Peccei–Quinn symmetry (1977) – introduction of a global U(1) symmetry
the spontaneous breaking of which leads to the vanishing of the QCD θ-parameter and explains the absence of CP violation in the strong interaction. Axion
arising as a pseudo-Goldstone boson
naturally becomes a candidate for cold dark matter.
Roberto D. Peccei.quote: If nature elegantly solves a problem, we should follow that hint.
Roberto D. Peccei.fun_fact: The term 'axion' was coined by Frank Wilczek, inspired by a detergent brand, because the new particle was supposed to 'clean up' the theory of the CP problem.

Helen Quinn.name: Helen Quinn
Helen Quinn.description: American theoretical physicist of Australian origin, best known for the joint development with Roberto Peccei of the Peccei–Quinn symmetry, which solves the strong CP problem. She made significant contributions to the understanding of fundamental symmetry breaking, axion properties, and to science education.
Helen Quinn.biography: Helen Rhoda Quinn was born in Australia in 1943. She received her PhD from Stanford University. She worked at Harvard University, SLAC (Stanford Linear Accelerator Center). She was the first woman president of the American Physical Society (APS). She has received many awards for leadership and contributions to particle physics.
key_discoveries: Peccei–Quinn symmetry (1977) – postulates the existence of a new symmetry

the spontaneous breaking of which links strong CP conservation with the pseudoscalar axion field. Quinn also participated in establishing mass constraints on the axion (Quinn–Weinberg bounds). Contribution to the systematics of fermion masses and interaction hierarchies in the Standard Model.
Helen Quinn.quote: Scientific questions have no boundaries, and the ability to ask them is a scientist's greatest gift.
Helen Quinn.fun_fact: Helen Quinn loved mathematics from childhood, but initially thought she would become a school teacher. A turning point was meeting a famous physicist who convinced her to pursue research.
